n100011: sn This chapter is closely linked to 1 Sam 31. It should be kept in mind that 1 and 2 Samuel were originally a single book, not separate volumes. Whereas in English Bible tradition the books of Samuel, Kings, Chronicles, and Ezra-Nehemiah are each regarded as two separate books, this was not the practice in ancient Hebrew tradition. Early canonical records, for example, counted them as single books respectively. The division into two books goes back to the Greek translation of the OT and was probably initiated because of the cumbersome length of copies due to the Greek practice (unlike that of Hebrew) of writing vowels. The present division into two books can be a little misleading in terms of perceiving the progression of the argument of the book; in some ways it is preferable to treat the books of 1-2 Samuel in a unified fashion.
n100012: sn The Amalekites were a nomadic people who inhabited Judah and the Transjordan. They are mentioned in Gen 36:15-16 as descendants of Amalek who in turn descended from Esau. In Exod 17:8-16 they are described as having acted in a hostile fashion toward Israel as the Israelites traveled to Canaan from Egypt. In DavidвҖҷs time the Amalekites were viewed as dangerous enemies who raided, looted, and burned Israelite cities (see 1 Sam 30).
n100013: sn Ziklag was a city in the Negev which had been given to David by Achish king of Gath. For more than a year David used it as a base from which he conducted military expeditions (see 1 Sam 27:5-12). According to 1 Sam 30:1-19, Ziklag was destroyed by the Amalekites while Saul fought the Philistines.
n100014: sn Tearing oneвҖҷs clothing and throwing dirt on oneвҖҷs head were outward expressions of grief in the ancient Near East, where such demonstrable reactions were a common response to tragic news.
n100015: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (the man mentioned at the beginning of v. 2) has been specified in the translation to avoid confusion as to who fell to the ground.
n100016: tn Heb вҖңhe fell to the ground and did obeisance.вҖқ
n1000110: tn In v. 2 he is called simply a вҖңman.вҖқ The word used here in v. 5 (so also in vv. 6, 13, 15), though usually referring to a young man or servant, may in this context designate a вҖңfightingвҖқ man, i.e., a soldier.
n1000111: tc Instead of the MT вҖңwho was recounting this to him, вҖҳHow do you know that Saul and his son Jonathan are dead?вҖҷвҖқ the Syriac Peshitta reads вҖңdeclare to me how Saul and his son Jonathan died.вҖқ
n1000112: tc The translation follows the Syriac Peshitta and one ms of the LXX; MT adds вҖңwho was telling him this.вҖқ
n1000113: tc The present translation reads with the Qere and many medieval Hebrew mss вҖңand I said,вҖқ rather than the Kethib which has вҖңand he said.вҖқ See the LXX, Syriac Peshitta, and Vulgate, all of which have the first person.
n1000114: tn As P. K. McCarter (II Samuel [AB], 59) points out, the Polel of the verb вҖҸЧһЧ•ЦјЧӘвҖҺ (mut, вҖңto dieвҖқ) вҖңrefers to dispatching or вҖҳfinishing offвҖҷ someone already wounded and near death.вҖқ Cf. NLT вҖңput me out of my misery.вҖқ
n1000115: tn Heb вҖңthe dizziness has seized me.вҖқ On the meaning of the Hebrew noun translated вҖңdizziness,вҖқ see P. K. McCarter, II Samuel (AB), 59-60. The point seems to be that he is unable to kill himself because he is weak and disoriented.
n1000116: tn The Hebrew text here is grammatically very awkward (Heb вҖңbecause all still my life in meвҖқ). Whether the broken construct phrase is due to the fact that the alleged speaker is in a confused state of mind as he is on the verge of dying, or whether the MT has sustained a defect in the transmission process, is not entirely clear. The former seems likely, although P. K. McCarter understands the MT to be the result of conflation of two shorter forms of text (P. K. McCarter, II Samuel [AB], 57, n. 9). Early translators also struggled with the verse, apparently choosing to leave part of the Hebrew text untranslated. For example, the Lucianic recension of the LXX lacks вҖңall,вҖқ while other witnesses (namely, one medieval Hebrew ms, codices A and B of the LXX, and the Syriac Peshitta) lack вҖңstill.вҖқ
n1000117: tn Heb вҖңafter his fallingвҖқ; NAB вҖңcould not survive his woundвҖқ; CEV вҖңwas too badly wounded to live much longer.вҖқ
n1000118: tc The MT lacks the definite article, but this is may be due to textual transmission error. It is preferable to read the вҖҸЧҗвҖҺ (alef) of вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧҰЦ°ЧўЦёЧ“ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (КҫetsКҝadah) as a вҖҸЧ”вҖҺ (he) giving вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧҰЦјЦ°ЧўЦёЧ“ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (hatseКҝadah). There is no reason to think that the soldier confiscated from SaulвҖҷs dead body only one of two or more bracelets that he was wearing (cf. NLT вҖңone of his braceletsвҖқ).
n1000119: sn The claims that the soldier is making here seem to contradict the story of SaulвҖҷs death as presented in 1 Sam 31:3-5. In that passage it appears that Saul took his own life, not that he was slain by a passerby who happened on the scene. Some scholars account for the discrepancy by supposing that conflicting accounts have been brought together in the MT. However, it is likely that the young man is here fabricating the account in a self-serving way so as to gain favor with David, or so he supposes. He probably had come across SaulвҖҷs corpse, stolen the crown and bracelet from the body, and now hopes to curry favor with David by handing over to him these emblems of SaulвҖҷs royalty. But in so doing the Amalekite greatly miscalculated DavidвҖҷs response to this alleged participation in SaulвҖҷs death. The consequence of his lies will instead be his own death.
n1000120: tc The present translation follows the Qere and many medieval Hebrew mss in reading вҖңhis garments,вҖқ rather than вҖңhis garment,вҖқ the reading of the Kethib.
n1000121: sn Hebrew has more than one word for foreigners. Since the Amalekites were obviously not Israelites and were вҖңinhabitants of the landвҖқ (1 Sam 27:8), adding the description ger (вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦөЧЁвҖҺ) must carry more significance than just вҖңforeignerвҖқ and вҖңresident.вҖқ In Mosaic Law the ger (вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦөЧЁвҖҺ) could join the covenant, be circumcised, offer sacrifices to the Lord, celebrate the festivals with Israel, were given equal protection under the law, and received some social welfare along with the Levites. (See notes at Exod 12:19 and Deut 29:11.) These ger (вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦөЧЁвҖҺ) appear to be converts or naturalized citizens with minimally different rights (they could not own land, just houses). The young man is probably positioning himself as someone loyal to Israel, consistent the description that he came from the camp of Saul/Israel (vss 2-3). He certainly would not want to be considered one of the Amalekites that David had just fought against (vs 1). This may also explain DavidвҖҷs expectation that he should know better than to slay the LordвҖҷs anointed (as SaulвҖҷs armor-bearer would not do in the true account in 1 Sam 31:4).
n1000123: tn Heb вҖңbe taught the bow.вҖқ The reference to вҖңthe bowвҖқ is very difficult here. Some interpreters (e.g., S. R. Driver, P. K. McCarter, Jr.) suggest deleting the word from the text (cf. NAB, TEV), but there does not seem to be sufficient evidence for doing so. Others (cf. KJV) understand the reference to be elliptical, meaning вҖңthe use of the bow.вҖқ The verse would then imply that with the deaths of Saul and Jonathan having occurred, a period of trying warfare is about to begin, requiring adequate preparation for war on the part of the younger generation. Various other views may also be found in the secondary literature. However, it seems best to understand the word here to be a reference to the name of a song (i.e., вҖңThe BowвҖқ), most likely the poem that follows in vv. 19-27 (cf. ASV, NASB, NRSV, CEV, NLT); NIV вҖңthis lament of the bow.вҖқ
n1000124: sn The Scroll of the Upright One (or The Book of Yashar) is a noncanonical writing which has not been preserved. Mentioned here and in Josh 10:12-13, it apparently was вҖңa collection of ancient national poetryвҖқ (so BDB 449 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЦёЧ©ЧҒЦёЧЁвҖҺ).
n1000125: sn The word beauty is used figuratively here to refer to Saul and Jonathan.
n1000126: sn The cities of Gath and Ashkelon are mentioned here by synecdoche of part for the whole. As major Philistine cities they in fact represent all of Philistia. The point is that when the sad news of fallen Israelite leadership reaches the Philistines, it will be for these enemies of Israel the occasion of great joy rather than grief.
n1000127: tc Instead of the MTвҖҷs вҖңfields of grain offeringsвҖқ the Lucianic recension of the LXX reads вҖңyour high places are mountains of death.вҖқ Cf. the Old Latin montes mortis (вҖңmountains of deathвҖқ).
n1000128: tn This is the only biblical occurrence of the Niphal of the verb вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦёЧўЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (gaКҝal). This verb usually has the sense of вҖңto abhorвҖқ or вҖңloathe.вҖқ But here it seems to refer to the now dirty and unprotected condition of a previously well-maintained instrument of battle.
n1000129: tc It is preferable to read here Hebrew вҖҸЧһЦёЧ©ЧҒЧ•ЦјЧ—Ц·вҖҺ (mashuakh) with many Hebrew mss, rather than вҖҸЧһЦёЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧҷЧ—Ц·вҖҺ (mashiakh) of the MT. Although the Syriac Peshitta understands the statement to pertain to Saul, the point here is not that Saul is not anointed. Rather, it is the shield of Saul that lies discarded and is no longer anointed. In ancient Near Eastern practice a warriorвҖҷs shield that was in normal use would have to be anointed regularly in order to ensure that the leather did not become dry and brittle. Like other warriors of his day Saul would have carefully maintained his tools of trade. But now that he is dead, the once-cared-for shield of the mighty warrior lies sadly discarded and woefully neglected, a silent but eloquent commentary on how different things are now compared to the way they were during SaulвҖҷs lifetime.
n1000130: tn The Hebrew imperfect verbal form is used here to indicate repeated past action.
n1000132: sn Clothing of scarlet was expensive and beyond the financial reach of most people.
n1000133: sn The expression weapons of war may here be a figurative way of referring to Saul and Jonathan.
n100021: tn Heb вҖңhe said.вҖқ The referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity and for stylistic reasons.
n100022: tc The expression вҖңthe cities of HebronвҖқ is odd; we would expect the noun to be in the singular, if used at all. Although the Syriac Peshitta has the expected reading вҖңin Hebron,вҖқ the MT is clearly the more difficult reading and should probably be retained here.
n100024: tn Heb вҖңand they told David.вҖқ The subject appears to be indefinite, allowing one to translate the verb as passive with David as subject.
n100028: tn Heb вҖңwill do with you this good.вҖқ
n1000210: sn The name Ish Bosheth means in Hebrew вҖңman of shame.вҖқ It presupposes an earlier form such as Ish Baal (вҖңman of the LordвҖқ), with the word вҖңbaalвҖқ being used of IsraelвҖҷs God. But because the Canaanite storm god was named вҖңBaal,вҖқ that part of the name was later replaced with the word вҖңshame.вҖқ
n1000211: tc The MT here reads вҖңthe Ashurite,вҖқ but this is problematic if it is taken to mean вҖңthe Assyrian.вҖқ Ish BoshethвҖҷs kingdom obviously was not of such proportions as to extend to Assyria. The Syriac Peshitta and the Vulgate render the word as вҖңthe Geshurite,вҖқ while the Targum has вҖңof the house of Ashur.вҖқ We should probably emend the Hebrew text to read вҖңthe Geshurite.вҖқ The Geshurites lived in the northeastern part of the land of Palestine.
n1000213: tn Heb вҖңAnd the number of the days in which David was king in Hebron over the house of Judah was seven years and six months.вҖқ
n1000214: tn Heb вҖңplay.вҖқ What is in view here is a gladiatorial contest in which representative groups of soldiers engage in mortal combat before the watching armies. Cf. NAB вҖңperform for usвҖқ; NASB вҖңhold (have NRSV) a contest before usвҖқ; NLT вҖңput on an exhibition of hand-to-hand combat.вҖқ
n1000216: tn Heb вҖңand they grabbed each one the head of his neighbor with his sword in the side of his neighbor and they fell together.вҖқ
n1000217: tn The meaning of the name вҖңHelkath HazzurimвҖқ (so NIV; KJV, NASB, NRSV similar) is not clear. BHK relates the name to the Hebrew term for вҖңside,вҖқ and this is reflected in NAB вҖңthe Field of the SidesвҖқ; the Greek OT revocalizes the Hebrew to mean something like вҖңField of Adversaries.вҖқ Cf. also TEV, NLT вҖңField of SwordsвҖқ; CEV вҖңField of Daggers.вҖқ
n1000220: tn Heb вҖңWhy should I strike you to the ground?вҖқ
n1000222: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (Asahel) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1000223: tn Heb вҖңthe.вҖқ The article functions here as a possessive pronoun.
n1000224: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (Asahel) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1000225: tn Heb вҖңhimвҖқ; the referent (Abner) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1000228: tn The Hebrew verb вҖҸЧ Ц·ЧўЦІЧңЦёЧ”вҖҺ (naКҝalah) used here is the Niphal perfect third person masculine singular of вҖҸЧўЦёЧңЦёЧ”вҖҺ (Кҝalah, вҖңto go upвҖқ). In the Niphal this verb вҖңis used idiomatically, of getting away from so as to abandonвҖҰespecially of an army raising a siegeвҖҰвҖқ (see S. R. Driver, Notes on the Hebrew Text and the Topography of the Books of Samuel, 244).
n1000230: tn Heb вҖңthey no longer chased after Israel and they no longer fought.вҖқ
n1000231: sn The rift valley is a large geographic feature extending from Galilee to the Gulf of Aqaba. Here only a section of the Jordan Valley is in view.
n1000232: tn The word вҖңRiverвҖқ is not in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n1000233: tn Heb вҖңand they went, all the Bitron.вҖқ The meaning of the Hebrew word вҖңBitron,вҖқ which is used only here in the OT, is disputed. The translation above follows BDB 144 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦҙЦјЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ in taking the word to be a proper name of an area east of the Jordan. A different understanding was advocated by W. R. Arnold, who took the word to refer to the forenoon or morning; a number of modern scholars and translations have adopted this view (cf. NAB, NASB, NRSV, CEV, NLT). See W. R. Arnold, вҖңThe Meaning of вҖҸЧ‘ЧӘЧЁЧ•ЧҹвҖҺ,вҖқ AJSL 28 (1911-1912): 274-83 and HALOT 167 s.v. In this case one could translate вҖңand they traveled all morning long.вҖқ
n100031: tn The Hebrew text does not have the word вҖңson.вҖқ So also in vv. 3-5.
n100033: tn The Hebrew text does not have вҖңsons.вҖқ
n100034: tn Heb вҖңwas strengthening himself.вҖқ The statement may have a negative sense here, perhaps suggesting that Abner was overstepping the bounds of political propriety in a self-serving way.
n100035: tc The Hebrew of the MT reads simply вҖңand he said,вҖқ with no expressed subject for the verb. It is not likely that the text originally had no expressed subject for this verb, since the antecedent is not immediately clear from the context. We should probably restore to the Hebrew text the name вҖңIsh Bosheth.вҖқ See a few medieval Hebrew mss, Aquila, Symmachus, Theodotion, and Vulgate. Perhaps the name was accidentally omitted by homoioarcton. Note that both the name Ish Bosheth and the following preposition вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧңвҖҺ (Кҫel) begin with the letter alef.
n100036: tn The expression вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЧ•Ц№Чҗ ЧҗЦ¶ЧңвҖҺ (boКҫ Кҫel) means вҖңcome toвҖқ or вҖңapproach,вҖқ but is also used as a euphemism for sexual relations.
n100037: sn This accusation against Abner is a very serious one, since an act of sexual infringement on the kingвҖҷs harem would probably have been understood as a blatant declaration of aspirations to kingship. As such it was not merely a matter of ethical impropriety but an act of grave political significance as well.
n1000310: tn Heb вҖңand you have laid upon me the guilt of the woman today.вҖқ
n1000311: tn Heb вҖңSo will God do to Abner and so he will add to him.вҖқ
n1000312: tc Heb вҖңhas sworn to David.вҖқ The LXX, with the exception of the recension of Origen, adds вҖңin this day.вҖқ
n1000313: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (Ish Bosheth) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1000314: tn The Hebrew text adds here, вҖңon his behalf.вҖқ
n1000315: tn Heb вҖңcut a covenant.вҖқ So also in vv. 13, 21.
n1000316: tn Heb вҖңand behold, my hand is with you.вҖқ
n1000317: tn The words вҖңwhen you come to see my face,вҖқ though found in the Hebrew text, are somewhat redundant given the similar expression in the earlier part of the verse. The words are absent from the Syriac Peshitta.
n1000318: tn Heb вҖңto Ish Bosheth son of Saul saying.вҖқ To avoid excessive sibilance (especially when read aloud) the translation renders вҖңsayingвҖқ as вҖңwith this demand.вҖқ
n1000321: tn In 1 Sam 25:44 this name appears as вҖңPalti.вҖқ
n1000323: tn Heb вҖңthe word of Abner was with.вҖқ
n1000324: tn Heb вҖңyou were seeking David to be king over you.вҖқ
n1000325: tc The present translation follows the LXX, the Syriac Peshitta, and Vulgate in reading вҖңI will save,вҖқ rather than the MT вҖңhe savedвҖқ or вҖңto save." The context calls for the first person common singular imperfect of the verb rather than the third person masculine singular perfect or the infinitive construct.
n1000328: tn Heb вҖңalso Abner went to speak into the ears of David in Hebron.вҖқ
n1000329: tn Heb вҖңall which was good in the eyes of Israel and in the eyes of all the house of Benjamin.вҖқ
n1000330: tn After the cohortatives, the prefixed verbal form with the prefixed conjunction indicates purpose or result.
n1000331: tn Heb вҖңAnd look, the servants of David.вҖқ
n1000332: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (David) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1000335: tn Heb вҖңyour going out and your coming in.вҖқ The expression is a merism. It specifically mentions the polar extremities of the actions but includes all activity in between the extremities as well, thus encompassing the entirety of oneвҖҷs activities.
n1000336: tn Heb вҖңand he struck him down there [in] the stomach.вҖқ
n1000337: tn Heb вҖңand he [i.e., Abner] died on account of the blood of Asahel his [i.e., JoabвҖҷs] brother.вҖқ
n1000338: tn Heb вҖңand may they whirl over.вҖқ In the Hebrew text the subject of the plural verb is unexpressed. The most likely subject is AbnerвҖҷs вҖңshed bloodвҖқ (v. 28), which is a masculine plural form in Hebrew. The verb вҖҸЧ—Ч•ЦјЧңвҖҺ (khul, вҖңwhirlвҖқ) is used with the preposition вҖҸЧўЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (Кҝal) only here and in Jer 23:19; 30:23.
n1000339: tc 4QSama has вҖңof JoabвҖқ rather than вҖңof his fatherвҖқ read by the MT.
n1000340: tn Heb вҖңthe house of Joab.вҖқ However, it is necessary to specify that DavidвҖҷs curse is aimed at JoabвҖҷs male descendants; otherwise it would not be clear that вҖңone who works at the spindleвҖқ refers to a man doing womanвҖҷs work rather than a woman.
n1000341: tn Heb вҖңand may there not be cut off from the house of Joab.вҖқ
n1000342: tn The expression used here is difficult. The translation вҖңone who works at the spindleвҖқ follows a suggestion of S. R. Driver that the expression pejoratively describes an effeminate man who, rather than being a mighty warrior, is occupied with tasks that are normally fulfilled by women (S. R. Driver, Notes on the Hebrew Text and the Topography of the Books of Samuel, 250-51; cf. NAB вҖңone unmanlyвҖқ; TEV вҖңfit only to do a womanвҖҷs workвҖқ; CEV вҖңcowardsвҖқ). But P. K. McCarter, following an alleged Phoenician usage of the noun to refer to вҖңcrutches,вҖқ adopts a different view. He translates the phrase вҖңclings to a crutch,вҖқ seeing here a further description of physical lameness (II Samuel [AB], 118). Such an idea fits the present context well and is followed by NIV, NCV, and NLT, although the evidence for this meaning is questionable. According to DNWSI 2:915-16, the noun consistently refers to a spindle in Phoenician, as it does in Ugaritic (see UT 468).
n1000344: tn A вҖ«вҖҸЧһЦҙЧҳЦёЦјЧ”вҖҺвҖ¬ (mittah) is typically bed with a frame (which can be ornate and covered with blankets and pillows). Here, like a stretcher, it is a portable frame for carrying a body, technically a bier.
n1000345: tn Heb вҖңlifted up his voice and wept.вҖқ The expression is a verbal hendiadys.
n1000346: tc The translation follows many medieval Hebrew manuscripts and several ancient versions in reading вҖңyour hands,вҖқ rather than вҖңyour hand.вҖқ
n1000347: tc 4QSama lacks the words вҖңall the people.вҖқ
n1000348: tn Heb вҖңThus God will do to me and thus he will add.вҖқ
n1000349: tn Heb вҖңit was good in their eyes.вҖқ
n1000351: tn Heb вҖңa leader and a great one.вҖқ The expression is a hendiadys.
n1000353: tn Heb вҖңMay the Lord repay the doer of the evil according to his evilвҖқ (NASB similar).
n100041: tn The MT does not specify the subject of the verb here, but the reference is to Ish Bosheth, so the name has been supplied in the translation for clarity. 4QSama and the LXX mistakenly read вҖңMephibosheth.вҖқ
n100043: tc The present translation, вҖңSaulвҖҷs son had two men,вҖқ is based on the reading вҖңto the son of Saul,вҖқ rather than the MTвҖҷs вҖңthe son of Saul.вҖқ The context requires the preposition to indicate the family relationship.
n100046: tc For the MTвҖҷs вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч”ЦөЧ ЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ (vehennah, вҖңand they,вҖқ feminine) read вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч”ЦҙЧ ЦјЦөЧ”вҖҺ (vehinneh, вҖңand beholdвҖқ). See the LXX, Syriac Peshitta, and Targum.
n100047:
tc The LXX (followed by NLT) reads, вҖңbehold the house doorkeeper was cleaning wheat and became drowsy and fell asleep and the brothers Rekcha and Baana avoided notice.вҖқ Josephus refers to the LXX with a slight expansion on the tradition in Ant. 7:48. The last sentence appears to follow the Hebrew MT, although understanding the final verb вҖҸЧ ЦҙЧһЦ°ЧңЦёЧҳЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (nimlatu; Niphal of вҖҸЧһЦёЧңЦ·ЧҳвҖҺ, malat) вҖңto escapeвҖқ as вҖңescaping noticeвҖқ is without parallel. The beginning of the verse in the LXX shares at least the words вҖңmidst of houseвҖқ and вҖңwheatвҖқ with the Hebrew MT. What sort of textual corruption through common scribal copying errors could lead to the different texts is unclear.
tn Heb вҖңtaking wheat.вҖқ The reference to getting wheat is obscure and traditionally inferred to mean that they came under the pretense of obtaining wheat (KJV, ESV, NASB, NIV, Holman). However the plausibility of this scenario is not culturally certain.
n100049: tn After the concluding disjunctive clause at the end of v. 6, the author now begins a more detailed account of the murder and its aftermath.
n1000410: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (Ish Bosheth) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1000411: tn Heb вҖңthey struck him down and killed him.вҖқ The expression is a verbal hendiadys.
n1000412: tn Heb вҖңand they removed his head.вҖқ The Syriac Peshitta and Vulgate lack these words.
n1000413: tc The Lucianic Greek recension lacks the words вҖңhis head.вҖқ
n1000414: sn The rift valley is a large geographic feature extending form Galilee to the gulf of Aqaba. The portion in view here runs along the west of the Dead Sea.
n1000416: tn Heb вҖңand he was like a bearer of good news in his eyes.вҖқ

n1000419: tn The antecedent of the pronoun вҖңthemвҖқ (which is not present in the Hebrew text, but implied) is not entirely clear. Presumably it is the corpses that were hung and not merely the detached hands and feet; cf. NIV вҖңhung the (their NRSV, NLT) bodiesвҖқ; the alternative is represented by TEV вҖңcut off their hands and feet, which they hung up.вҖқ
n1000421: tc The LXX adds вҖңthe son of NerвҖқ by conformity with common phraseology elsewhere.

n100058: tn The Hebrew text has вҖңheвҖқ rather than вҖңthe Jebusites.вҖқ The referent has been specified in the translation for clarity. In the Syriac Peshitta and some mss of the Targum the verb is plural rather than singular.
n100059: tc There is some confusion among the witnesses concerning this word. The Kethib is the Qal perfect third common plural вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦёЧ Ц°ЧҗЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (saneКҫu, вҖңthey hatedвҖқ), referring to the JebusitesвҖҷ attitude toward David. The Qere is the Qal passive participle construct plural вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦ°Ч Ц»ЧҗЦөЧҷвҖҺ (senuКҫe, вҖңhatedвҖқ), referring to DavidвҖҷs attitude toward the Jebusites. 4QSama has the Qal perfect third person feminine singular вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦёЧ Ц°ЧҗЦёЧ”вҖҺ (saneКҫah, вҖңhatedвҖқ), the subject of which would be вҖңthe soul of David.вҖқ The difference is minor and the translation adopted above works for either the Kethib or the Qere.
n1000510:
tn The meaning of the Hebrew term has been debated. For a survey of various views, see P. K. McCarter, II Samuel (AB), 139-40.
sn If a water tunnel is in view here, it is probably the so-called WarrenвҖҷs Shaft that extends up from HezekiahвҖҷs tunnel. It would have provided a means for surprise attack against the occupants of the City of David. The LXX seems not to understand the reference here, translating вҖңby the water shaftвҖқ as вҖңwith a small knife.вҖқ
n1000511: tn Heb вҖңthe house.вҖқ TEV takes this as a reference to the temple (вҖңthe LordвҖҷs houseвҖқ).
n1000512: tc 4QSama and the LXX lack the word вҖңGod,вҖқ probably due to harmonization with the more common biblical phrase вҖңthe Lord of hosts.вҖқ
n1000513: tn Traditionally, вҖңthe Lord God of hostsвҖқ (KJV, NASB); NIV, NLT вҖңthe Lord God AlmightyвҖқ; CEV вҖңthe Lord (+ God NCV) All-Powerful.вҖқ
n1000514: tn The translation assumes that the disjunctive clause is circumstantial-causal, giving the reason for DavidвҖҷs success.
n1000519: tn The infinitive absolute lends emphasis to the following verb.
n1000520: tn The name means вҖңLord of the outbursts.вҖқ

n1000525: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1000526: tn Some translate as вҖңbalsam treesвҖқ (cf. NASB, NIV, NRSV, NJB, NLT); cf. KJV, NKJV, ASV вҖңmulberry treesвҖқ; NAB вҖңmastic treesвҖқ; NEB, REB вҖңaspens.вҖқ The exact identification of the type of tree or plant is uncertain.
n1000528: tn Heb вҖңfrom Gibeon until you enter Gezer.вҖқ
n100061: tn The translation understands the verb to be a defective spelling of вҖҸЧ•Ц·ЧҷЦјЦ¶ЧҗЦұЧЎЦ№ЧЈвҖҺ (vayyeКҫesof) due to quiescence of the letter вҖҸЧҗвҖҺ (alef). The root therefore is вҖҸЧҗЦёЧЎЦ·ЧЈвҖҺ (Кҫasaf, вҖңto gatherвҖқ). The Masoretes, however, pointed the verb as вҖҸЧ•Ц·ЧҷЦјЦ№ЧЎЦ¶ЧЈвҖҺ (vayyosef), understanding it to be a form of вҖҸЧҷЦёЧЎЦ·ЧЈвҖҺ (yasaf, вҖңto addвҖқ). This does not fit the context, which calls for a verb of gathering.
n100064: tn Heb вҖңfrom,вҖқ but the following context indicates they traveled to this location.
n100065: tn This is another name for Kiriath Jearim (see 1 Chr 13:6).
n100066: tc The MT has here a double reference to the name (вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦөЧқ Ч©ЧҒЦөЧқвҖҺ, shem shem). Many medieval Hebrew mss in the first occurrence point the word differently and read the adverb вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧқвҖҺ (sham, вҖңthereвҖқ). This is also the understanding of the Syriac Peshitta (Syr., taman). While this yields an acceptable understanding to the text, it is more likely that the MT reading results from dittography. If the word did occur twice, one might have expected the first occurrence to have the article. The present translation therefore reads вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦөЧқвҖҺ only once.
n100069: tc Heb вҖңwere celebrating before the Lord with all woods of firвҖқ (cf. KJV, ASV, NASB). If the text is retained, the last expression must be elliptical, referring to musical instruments made from fir wood. But it is preferable to emend the text in light of 1 Chr 13:8, which reads вҖңwere celebrating before the Lord with all strength and with songs.вҖқ
n1000611: tn That is, вҖңsistrumsвҖқ (so NAB, NIV); ASV, NASB, NRSV, CEV, NLT вҖңcastanets.вҖқ

n1000619: tn Heb вҖңbecause the Lord broke out [with] a breaking out [i.e., an outburst] against Uzzah.вҖқ

n1000622: tn Heb вҖңand it was told to King David, saying.вҖқ

n1000624: tn Heb вҖңand David was dancing with all his strength before the Lord, and David was girded with a linen ephod.вҖқ

n1000627: tn The Hebrew text adds вҖңin her heart.вҖқ Cf. CEV вҖңshe was disgusted (+ with him TEV)вҖқ; NLT вҖңwas filled with contempt for himвҖқ; NCV вҖңshe hated him.вҖқ
n1000628: tc The Syriac Peshitta lacks вҖңin its place.вҖқ
n1000629: tn Heb вҖңto all the people, to all the throng of Israel.вҖқ
n1000630: tn The Hebrew word used here вҖҸЧҗЦ¶Ч©ЧҒЦ°ЧӨЦјЦёЧЁвҖҺ (Кҫespar) is found in the OT only here and in the parallel passage found in 1 Chr 16:3. Its exact meaning is uncertain, although the context indicates that it was a food of some sort (cf. KJV вҖңa good piece of fleshвҖқ; NRSV вҖңa portion of meatвҖқ). The translation adopted here (вҖңdate cakeвҖқ) follows the lead of the Greek translations of the LXX, Aquila, and Symmachus (cf. NASB, NIV, NLT).
n1000631: tn Heb вҖңand all the people went, each to his house.вҖқ

n1000636: tn Heb вҖңall his houseвҖқ; CEV вҖңanyone else in your family.вҖқ

n100074: tc Several medieval Hebrew mss and the Syriac Peshitta lack this word.
n100075: tn Heb вҖңall that is in your heart.вҖқ

n100078: tn Heb вҖңtribes of IsraelвҖқ (so KJV, NASB, NCV), but the parallel passage in 1 Chr 17:6 has вҖңjudges of Israel.вҖқ

n1000712: tn Heb вҖңand I will make for you a great name like the name of the great ones who are in the earth.вҖқ
n1000717: tn In the Hebrew text the verb is apparently perfect with vav consecutive, which would normally suggest a future sense (вҖңhe will declareвҖқ; so the LXX, бјҖПҖОұОіОіОөО»Оөбҝ– [apangelei]). But the context seems instead to call for a present or past nuance (вҖңhe declaresвҖқ or вҖңhe has declaredвҖқ). The synoptic passage in 1 Chr 17:10 has вҖҸЧ•ЦёЧҗЦ·Ч’ЦјЦҙЧ“вҖҺ (vaКҫaggid, вҖңand I declaredвҖқ). The construction used in 2 Sam 7:11 highlights this important statement.
n1000719: tn Heb вҖңhouse,вҖқ but used here in a metaphorical sense, referring to a royal dynasty. Here the LordвҖҷs use of the word plays off the literal sense that David had in mind as he contemplated building a temple for the Lord. To reflect this in the English translation the adjective вҖңdynasticвҖқ has been supplied.
n1000720: tn Heb, вҖңwhen your days are full and you lie down with your ancestors.вҖқ
n1000721: tn Heb вҖңyour seed after you who comes out from your insides.вҖқ
n1000722: tn Heb вҖңand I will establish the throne of his kingdom permanently.вҖқ
n1000723: tc Heb вҖңbefore you.вҖқ A few medieval Hebrew mss read instead вҖңbefore me,вҖқ which makes better sense contextually. (See also the LXX and the Syriac Peshitta.) The MT reading is probably the result of dittography (note the вҖҸЧӣвҖҺ [kaf] at the beginning of the next form), with the extra вҖҸЧӣвҖҺ then being interpreted as a pronominal suffix.
n1000725: tn Heb вҖңaccording to all these words and according to all this revelation, so Nathan said to David.вҖқ
n1000727: tn Heb вҖңand this was small in your eyes, O Lord God, so you spoke concerning the house of your servant for a distance.вҖқ
n1000728: tn Heb вҖңand this [is] the law of manвҖқ; KJV вҖңis this the manner of man, O Lord God?вҖқ; NAB вҖңthis too you have shown to manвҖқ; NRSV вҖңMay this be instruction for the people, O Lord God!вҖқ This part of the verse is very enigmatic; no completely satisfying solution has yet been suggested. The present translation tries to make sense of the MT by understanding the phrase as a question that underscores the uniqueness of GodвҖҷs dealings with David as described here. The parallel passage in 1 Chr 17:17 reads differently (see the note there).
n1000729: tn Heb вҖңand you know your servant.вҖқ The verb here refers to recognizing another in a special way and giving them special treatment (see 1 Chr 17:18). Some English versions take this to refer to the LordвҖҷs knowledge of David himself: CEV вҖңyou know my thoughtsвҖқ; NLT вҖңknow what I am really like.вҖқ
n1000730: tn Heb вҖңfor the sake of your word and according to your heart.вҖқ
n1000732: tn Heb вҖңin all which we heard with our ears.вҖқ The phrase translated вҖңin allвҖқ вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°ЧӣЦ№ЧңвҖҺ (bekhol) should probably be emended to вҖңaccording to allвҖқ вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ°ЧӣЦ№ЧңвҖҺ (kekhol).
n1000734: tn Heb вҖңwhose GodвҖқ or вҖңbecause God.вҖқ In the Hebrew text this clause is subordinated to what precedes. The clauses are separated in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n1000735: tn The verb is plural in Hebrew, agreeing grammatically with the divine name, which is a plural of degree.
n1000737: tn Heb вҖңand to do for you [plural form] the great [thing] and awesome [things] for your land.вҖқ
n1000738: tn Heb вҖңfrom Egypt, nations and their gods.вҖқ The LXX has вҖңnations and tents,вҖқ which reflects a mistaken metathesis of letters in вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧңЦ№Ч”ЦёЧҷЧ•вҖҺ (Кҫelohayv, вҖңits godsвҖқ) and вҖҸЧҗЦ№Ч”ЦёЧңЦёЧҷЧ•вҖҺ (Кҫohalayv, вҖңits tentsвҖқ).
n1000739: tn Heb вҖңand you established for yourself your people Israel for yourself for a people permanently.вҖқ
n1000740: tn Heb вҖңand now, O Lord God, the word which you spoke concerning your servant and concerning his house, establish permanently.вҖқ
n1000742: tn Heb вҖңand your name might be great permanently.вҖқ Following the imperative in v. 23b, the prefixed verbal form with vav conjunctive indicates purpose/result.
n1000743: tn Heb вҖңsaying.вҖқ The words вҖңas peopleвҖқ are supplied in the translation for clarification and stylistic reasons.
n1000744: tn Heb вҖңthe house.вҖқ See the note on вҖңdynastic houseвҖқ in the following verse.
n1000746: tn Heb вҖңa house.вҖқ This maintains the wordplay from v. 11 (see the note on the word вҖңhouseвҖқ there) and is continued in v. 29.
n1000748: tn Heb вҖңthe God.вҖқ The article indicates uniqueness here.
n1000749: tn The translation understands the prefixed verb form as a jussive, indicating DavidвҖҷs wish/prayer. Another option is to take the form as an imperfect and translate вҖңyour words are true.вҖқ
n1000750: tn Heb вҖңand you have spoken to your servant this good thing.вҖқ
n1000751: tn Heb вҖңhouseвҖқ (again later in this verse). See the note on вҖңdynastic houseвҖқ in v. 27.
n1000752: tn Or вҖңpermanentlyвҖқ; cf. NLT вҖңit is an eternal blessing.вҖқ
n100081: tn Heb вҖңthe bridle of one cubit.вҖқ Many English versions treat this as a place name because the parallel text in 1 Chr 18:1 reads вҖңGathвҖқ (which is used by NLT here). It is possible that вҖңthe bridle of one cubitвҖқ is to be understood as вҖңthe token of surrender,вҖқ referring to the PhilistineвҖҷs defeat rather than a specific place (cf. TEV, CEV).
n100082: tn Heb вҖңfrom the hand [i.e., control] of the Philistines.вҖқ
n100083: tn Heb вҖңand he measured [with] two [lengths] of rope to put to death and [with] the fullness of the rope to keep alive.вҖқ
n100084: tn Heb вҖңand the Moabites were servants of David, carriers of tribute.вҖқ
n100085: tc The LXX has бјҗПҖО№ПғП„бҝҶПғОұО№ (epistД“sai, вҖңcause to standвҖқ). See the parallel text in 1 Chr 18:3.
n100087: tn The MT does not have the name вҖңEuphratesвҖқ in the text. It is supplied in the margin (Qere) as one of ten places where the Masoretes believed that something was вҖңto be read although it was not writtenвҖқ in the text as they had received it. The ancient versions (LXX, Syriac Peshitta, Vulgate) include the word. See also the parallel text in 1 Chr 18:3.
n100088: tc The LXX has вҖң1,000 chariots and 7,000 charioteers,вҖқ a reading adopted in the text of the NIV. See the parallel text in 1 Chr 18:4.
n100089: tn Heb вҖңand David cut the hamstrings of all the chariot horses, and he left from them 100 chariot horses.вҖқ
n1000812: tc The LXX includes seventeen words (in Greek) at the end of v. 7 that are not found in the MT. The LXX addition is as follows: вҖңAnd Sousakim king of Egypt took them when he came up to Jerusalem in the days of Rehoboam the son of Solomon.вҖқ This Greek reading now finds Hebrew support in 4QSama. For a reconstruction of this poorly preserved Qumran text see E. C. Ulrich, Jr., The Qumran Text of Samuel and Josephus (HSM), 45-48.
n1000813: tn Heb вҖңBetahвҖқ (so KJV, NASB, NRSV), but the name should probably be corrected to вҖңTebah.вҖқ See the parallel text in 1 Chr 18:8.
n1000814: tn The name is spelled вҖңTouвҖқ in the parallel text in 1 Chr 18:9. NIV adopts the spelling вҖңTouвҖқ here.
n1000815: tn Heb вҖңToi.вҖқ The proper name has been replaced by the pronoun in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n1000816: tn The name appears as вҖңHadoramвҖқ in the parallel text in 1 Chr 18:10.
n1000817: tn Heb вҖңto ask concerning him for peace.вҖқ
n1000818: tn Heb вҖңand to bless him because he fought with Hadadezer and defeated him, for Hadadezer was a man of battles with Toi.вҖқ
n1000819: tn Heb вҖңand in his hand were items of silver and items of gold and items of bronze.вҖқ
n1000820: tn Heb вҖңalso them King David made holy to the Lord.вҖқ
n1000821: tn Heb вҖңwith the silver and the gold that he had dedicated from.вҖқ
n1000823: tc Heb вҖңAram.вҖқ A few Hebrew mss along with the LXX and Syriac read вҖңEdomвҖқ (cf. 2 Sam 8:14 and 1 Chr 18:11). Aram and Edom are spelled similarly, the difference being a вҖҸЧ“вҖҺ (dalet) vs. a вҖҸЧЁвҖҺ (resh). Besides the textual witnesses, the geography in v. 13, the Valley of Salt, fits Edom and not Aram.
n1000826: tc See the note on вҖңAramвҖқ in v. 12.
n1000827: tn The words вҖңhe defeatedвҖқ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n1000828: tc The MT is repetitious here: вҖңHe placed in Edom garrisons; in all Edom he placed garrisons.вҖқ The Vulgate lacks вҖңin all EdomвҖқ; most of the Greek tradition (with the exception of the Lucianic recension and the recension of Origen) and the Syriac Peshitta lack вҖңhe placed garrisons.вҖқ The MT reading appears here to be the result of a conflation of variant readings.
n1000829: tn Heb вҖңand David was doing what is just and fair for all his people.вҖқ
n1000831: tc Here Ahimelech is called вҖңthe son of Abiathar,вҖқ but NCV, CEV, and REB reverse this to conform with 1 Sam 22:20. Most recent English versions (e.g., NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT) retain the order found in the MT.
n1000832: tc The translation follows the Syriac Peshitta, Targum, and Vulgate in reading вҖңover,вҖқ rather than the simple conjunction that appears in MT. See also the parallel passage in 1 Chr 18:17.
n1000833: sn That DavidвҖҷs sons could have been priests, in light of the fact that they were not of the priestly lineage, is strange. One must assume either (1) that the word вҖңpriestвҖқ (вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ№Ч”ЦөЧҹвҖҺ, kohen) during this period of time could be used in a broader sense of вҖңchief rulerвҖқ (KJV); вҖңchief ministerвҖқ (ASV, NASB), or вҖңroyal adviserвҖқ (NIV), perhaps based on the parallel passage in 1 Chr 18:17 which has вҖңthe kingвҖҷs leading officialsвҖқ, or (2) that in DavidвҖҷs day members of the kingвҖҷs family could function as a special category of вҖңpriestsвҖқ (cf. NLT вҖңpriestly leadersвҖқ). The latter option seems to be the more straightforward way of understanding the word in 2 Sam 8:18.
n100091: sn 2 Samuel 9-20 is known as the Succession Narrative. It is a literary unit that describes DavidвҖҷs efforts at consolidating his own kingdom following the demise of King Saul; it also provides the transition to subsequent leadership on the part of DavidвҖҷs successor Solomon.
n100097: tn Heb вҖңhe fell on his face and bowed down.вҖқ
n100099: tn Heb вҖңand you will eat food at my table continually.вҖқ
n1000910: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (Mephibosheth) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1000911: tn Heb вҖңWhat is your servant, that you turn to a dead dog which is like me?вҖқ
n1000913: tn The Hebrew text implies, but does not actually contain, the words вҖңits produceвҖқ here.
n1000914: tc The words вҖңit will be,вҖқ though present in the MT, are absent from the LXX, the Syriac Peshitta, and Vulgate.
n1000917: tc Heb вҖңmy table.вҖқ But the first person reference to David is awkward here since the quotation of DavidвҖҷs words has already been concluded in v. 10; nor does the вҖңmyвҖқ refer to Ziba, since the latter part of v. 11 does not seem to be part of ZibaвҖҷs response to the king. The ancient versions are not unanimous in the way that they render the phrase. The LXX has вҖңthe table of DavidвҖқ (П„бҝҶПӮ П„ПҒОұПҖОӯО¶О·ПӮ О”ОұП…О№Оҙ, tД“s trapezД“s Dauid); the Syriac Peshitta has вҖңthe table of the kingвҖқ (patureh demalkaКҫ); the Vulgate has вҖңyour tableвҖқ (mensam tuam). The present translation follows the LXX.
n100104: tn Heb вҖңand David sent to console him by the hand of his servants concerning his father.вҖқ
n100105: tn Heb вҖңIs David honoring your father in your eyes when he sends to you ones consoling?вҖқ
n100106: tn Heb вҖңIs it not to explore the city and to spy on it and to overthrow it [that] David has sent his servants to you?вҖқ
n100107: tn Heb вҖңand he cut their robes in the middle unto their buttocks.вҖқ
n100108: tn Heb вҖңtheyвҖқ; the referent (the messengers) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n100109: tn The words вҖңwhat had happenedвҖқ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n1001011: tn Heb вҖңthat they were a stench [i.e., disgusting] with David.вҖқ
n1001013: tn Or вҖңArameans of Beth Rehob and Arameans of Zobah.вҖқ
n1001014: tn Or perhaps вҖңthe men of Tob.вҖқ The ancient versions (the LXX, the Syriac Peshitta, and Vulgate) understand the name to be вҖңIsh Tob.вҖқ It is possible that вҖңIshвҖқ is dittographic and that we should read simply вҖңTob,вҖқ a reading adopted by a number of recent English versions.
n1001015: tn The words вҖңthe newsвҖқ and вҖңto meet themвҖқ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons and for clarification.
n1001016: tn Heb вҖңand Joab saw that the face of the battle was to him before and behind and he chose from all the best in Israel and arranged to meet Aram.вҖқ
n1001019: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (Joab) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

n1001021: tn Heb вҖңif the sons of Ammon are stronger than you.вҖқ
n1001022: tn Heb вҖңand the Lord will do what is good in his eyes.вҖқ
n1001023: tn Heb вҖңand the army which was with him.вҖқ
n1001024: tn Heb вҖңand Joab returned from against the sons of Ammon and entered.вҖқ
n1001027: tn Heb вҖңand Hadadezer sent and brought out Aram which is.вҖқ
n1001028: tn Heb вҖңfrom beyond the River.вҖқ The name вҖңEuphratesвҖқ has been supplied in the translation for clarity.
n1001030: tn The word вҖңRiverвҖқ is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied in the translation for clarity.
n1001031: tn Heb вҖңhorsemenвҖқ (so KJV, NASB, NCV, NRSV, NLT) but the Lucianic recension of the LXX reads вҖңfoot soldiers,вҖқ as does the parallel text in 1 Chr 19:18. Cf. NAB, NIV.
n100111: tc Codex Leningrad (B19A), on which BHS is based, has here вҖңmessengersвҖқ (вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧһЦјЦ·ЧңЦ°ЧҗЦёЧӣЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ, hammalКҫakhim), probably as the result of contamination from the occurrence of that word in v. 4. The present translation follows most Hebrew mss and the ancient versions, which read вҖңkingsвҖқ (вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧһЦјЦ¶ЧңЦёЧҗЧӣЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ, hammelakhim).
n100115: tn The disjunctive clause contrasts DavidвҖҷs inactivity with the armyвҖҷs activity.
n100116: tn Heb вҖңon the roof of the house of the king.вҖқ So also in vv. 8, 9.
n100117: tn The disjunctive clause highlights this observation and builds the tension of the story.
n100118: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (the messenger) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n100119: tn Heb вҖңand David sent messengers and he took her.вҖқ
n1001110: tn The expression вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЧ•Ц№Чҗ ЧҗЦ¶ЧңвҖҺ (boКҫ Кҫel) means вҖңcome toвҖқ or вҖңapproach,вҖқ but is also used as a euphemism for sexual relations, the implied purpose for approaching someone. Here it refers only to the stage of approaching while the next verb describes the result. That she is the subject of this verb (while David is the subject of the next verb) probably indicates that the act was consensual.
n1001111: tn Heb вҖңhe lay down with her.вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧ©ЦёЧҒЧӣЦ·Ч‘вҖҺ (shakav) вҖңto lie downвҖқ can be a euphemism for going to bed for sexual relations.
n1001112: tn The parenthetical disjunctive clause further heightens the tension by letting the reader know that Bathsheba, having just completed her menstrual cycle, is ripe for conception. See P. K. McCarter, II Samuel (AB), 286. Since she just had her period, it will also be obvious to those close to the scene that Uriah, who has been away fighting, cannot be the father of the child.
n1001113: tn Heb вҖңconcerning the peace of Joab and concerning the peace of the people and concerning the peace of the battle.вҖқ
n1001115: tn Heb вҖңand there went out after him the gift of the king.вҖқ
n1001116: tc The Lucianic recension of the Old Greek translation lacks the word вҖңall.вҖқ
n1001117: tn Heb вҖңlie with.вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧ©ЦёЧҒЧӣЦ·Ч‘вҖҺ (shakav) вҖңto lie downвҖқ can be a euphemism for going to bed for sexual relations.
n1001118: tn Heb вҖңas you live and as your soul lives.вҖқ
n1001119: tn On the chronology involved here see P. K. McCarter, II Samuel (AB), 287.
n1001120: tn Heb вҖңthe valiant men.вҖқ This refers in context to the strongest or most valiant defenders of the city Joab and the Israelite army were besieging, so the present translation uses вҖңthe best enemy soldiersвҖқ for clarity.
n1001121: tn Heb вҖңsome of the people from the servants of David.вҖқ
n1001122: tn Heb вҖңJoab sent and related to David all the matters of the battle.вҖқ
n1001123: sn The upper millstone (Heb вҖңmillstone of ridingвҖқ) refers to the heavy circular stone that was commonly rolled over a circular base in order to crush and grind such things as olives.
n1001126: tc The translation follows the Qere (вҖңyour servantsвҖқ) rather than the Kethib (вҖңyour servantвҖқ).
n1001127: tn Heb вҖңlet not this matter be evil in your eyes.вҖқ
n1001128: tn Heb вҖңaccording to this and according to this the sword devours.вҖқ
n1001130: tn The Hebrew text does not have вҖңwith these words.вҖқ They are supplied in the translation for clarity and for stylistic reasons.
n1001132: tn Heb вҖңDavid sent and gathered her to his house.вҖқ
n1001133: tn Heb вҖңand the thing which David had done was evil in the eyes of the Lord.вҖқ Note the verbal connection with v. 25. Though David did not regard the matter as evil, the Lord certainly did.
n100121: tc A few medieval Hebrew mss, the LXX, and the Syriac Peshitta add вҖңthe prophet.вҖқ The words are included in a few modern English version (e.g., TEV, CEV, NLT).
n100122: tn Heb вҖңhimвҖқ; the referent (David) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n100123: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (Nathan) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n100124: tn The Hebrew text repeats вҖңto him.вҖқ
n100126: tn The three Hebrew imperfect verbal forms in this sentence have a customary nuance; they describe past actions that were repeated or typical.
n100128: tn Heb вҖңand on his chest [or perhaps, вҖңlapвҖқ] it would lie.вҖқ
n100129: tn Heb вҖңcame to the rich man.вҖқ In the translation вҖңarrived at the rich manвҖҷs homeвҖқ has been used for stylistic reasons.
n1001210: tn Heb вҖңand he refused to take from his flock and from his herd to prepare [a meal] for.вҖқ
n1001211: tn Heb вҖңwho had come to himвҖқ (also a second time later in this verse). The word вҖңvisitвҖқ has been supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons and for clarity.
n1001213: tn Heb вҖңthe man doing this [is] a son of death.вҖқ See 1 Sam 20:31 for another use of this expression, which must mean вҖңhe is as good as deadвҖқ or вҖңhe deserves to die,вҖқ as 1 Sam 20:32 makes clear.
n1001214:
tc With the exception of the Lucianic recension, the Old Greek translation has here вҖңsevenfoldвҖқ rather than вҖңfourfold,вҖқ a reading that S. R. Driver thought probably to be the original reading (S. R. Driver, Notes on the Hebrew Text and the Topography of the Books of Samuel, 291). However, Exod 22:1 [21:37 HT] specifies fourfold repayment for a stolen sheep, which is consistent with 2 Sam 12:6. Some mss of the Targum and the Syriac Peshitta exaggerate the idea to вҖңfortyfold.вҖқ
tn Heb вҖңthe lamb he must repay fourfold because he did this thing and because he did not have compassion.вҖқ
n1001216: tn Heb вҖңand the wives of your lord into your chest [or вҖңlapвҖқ].вҖқ The words вҖңI putвҖқ are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons and for clarification.
n1001217: tn Or вҖңword, message.вҖқ The вҖңword of the LordвҖқ sometimes refers to a prophetic message from God and sometimes to his past revelation. Here it refers to the LordвҖҷs laws which David has violated.
n1001220: tn Heb вҖңhouseвҖқ (so NAB, NRSV); NCV, TEV, CEV вҖңfamily.вҖқ
n1001222: tn Heb вҖңwill lie down with.вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧ©ЦёЧҒЧӣЦ·Ч‘вҖҺ (shakhav) вҖңto lie downвҖқ can be a euphemism for going to bed for sexual relations.
n1001223: tn Heb вҖңin the eyes of this sun.вҖқ
n1001226: tc The MT has here вҖңbecause you have caused the enemies of the Lord to treat the Lord with such contempt.вҖқ This is one of the so-called tiqqune sopherim, or вҖңemendations of the scribes.вҖқ According to this ancient tradition, the scribes changed the text in order to soften somewhat the negative light in which David was presented. If that is the case, the MT reflects the altered text. The present translation departs from the MT here. Elsewhere the Piel stem of this verb means вҖңtreat with contempt,вҖқ but never вҖңcause someone to treat with contempt.вҖқ
n1001227: tn Heb вҖңand the Lord struck the childвҖҰand he was ill.вҖқ It is necessary to repeat вҖңthe childвҖқ in the translation to make clear who became ill, since вҖңthe Lord struck the child that UriahвҖҷs wife had borne to David, and he became very illвҖқ could be understood to mean that David himself became ill.
n1001230: tn The three Hebrew verbs that follow in this verse are perfects with prefixed vav. They may describe repeated past actions or actions which accompanied DavidвҖҷs praying and fasting.
n1001232: tn Heb вҖңhe will do harm.вҖқ The object is not stated in the Hebrew text. The statement may be intentionally vague, meaning that he might harm himself or them!
n1001233: tn Heb вҖңDavid.вҖқ The name has been replaced in the translation by the pronoun (вҖңheвҖқ) for stylistic reasons.
n1001234: tc For the MT вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ·ЧўЦІЧ‘Ч•ЦјЧЁвҖҺ (baКҝavur, вҖңfor the sake ofвҖқ) we should probably read вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°ЧўЧ•Ц№Ч“вҖҺ (beКҝod, вҖңwhileвҖқ). See the Lucianic Greek recension, the Syriac Peshitta, and the Targum.
n1001237: tn The combination of the verb вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЧ•Ц№ЧҗвҖҺ (boКҫ; вҖңto come, enterвҖқ) and the preposition вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧңвҖҺ (Кҫel; вҖңtoвҖқ) means вҖңto approach, to come toвҖқ (HALOT 1:113). This common expression is also used as a euphemism for coming together for sexual relations. Although some take the phrase to be a graphic depiction of a man actions in sexual relations with a woman, certain factors clarify that it is a euphemism. First, the phrase also describes a woman approaching a man for sexual relations (2 Sam 11:4), a situation where this phrase cannot be explicitly descriptive. Second, the phrase is paired here with вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧӣЦ·Ч‘вҖҺ (shakhav), вҖңto lie down,вҖқ which only makes sense if the two are complementary (compare also Gen 19:33-34 which uses both verbs of LotвҖҷs daughters, but without the preposition). The verb вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧӣЦ·Ч‘вҖҺ can imply lying down for sleep or for sexual relations. When вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЧ•Ц№Чҗ ЧҗЦ¶ЧңвҖҺ (boКҫ Кҫel) is used with вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧӣЦ·Ч‘вҖҺ (shakhav), they state the natural progression of approaching and then lying with. Hebrew can use the two together, or either separately, as a euphemism for sexual relations. But if the phrase вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЧ•Ц№Чҗ ЧҗЦ¶ЧңвҖҺ were already an explicit depiction of sex, then the latter phrase with вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧӣЦ·Ч‘вҖҺ, вҖңto lie with,вҖқ would be pointless. So 2 Sam 11:4 and 2 Sam 12:24 are important evidence for how this phrase really works, and it is appropriate to also use euphemisms in translation.
n1001238: tn Heb вҖңand he lay with her.вҖқ The phrase is a euphemism for sexual relations.
n1001239:
tc The Kethiv reads вҖңhe namedвҖқ while the Qere reads вҖңshe named.вҖқ
tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (David) has been specified in the translation for clarity. While some translations render the pronoun as third person plural (вҖңtheyвҖқ), implying that both David and Bathsheba together named the child, it is likely that the name вҖңSolomon,вҖқ which is related to the Hebrew word for вҖңpeaceвҖқ (and may be derived from it) had special significance for David, who would have regarded the birth of a second child to Bathsheba as a confirming sign that God had forgiven his sin and was at peace with him.
n1001241: sn The name Jedidiah means вҖңloved by the Lord.вҖқ
n1001242: sn Here the narrative resumes the battle story that began in 11:1 (see 11:25). The author has interrupted that story to give the related account of DavidвҖҷs sin with Bathsheba and the murder of Uriah. He now returns to the earlier story and brings it to a conclusion.
n1001243: sn The expression translated the water supply of the city (Heb вҖңthe city of the watersвҖқ) apparently refers to that part of the fortified city that guarded the water supply of the entire city. Joab had already captured this part of the city, but he now defers to King David for the capture of the rest of the city. In this way the king will receive the credit for this achievement.
n1001245: tn Part of the Greek tradition wrongly understands Hebrew вҖҸЧһЦ·ЧңЦ°ЧӣЦјЦёЧқвҖҺ (malkam, вҖңtheir kingвҖқ) as a proper name (вҖңMilcomвҖқ). Some English versions follow the Greek here, rendering the phrase вҖңthe crown of MilcomвҖқ (so NRSV; cf. also NAB, CEV). TEV takes this as a reference not to the Ammonite king but to вҖңthe idol of the Ammonite god Molech.вҖқ
n1001246: tn Heb вҖңand its weight [was] a talent of gold.вҖқ The weight of this ornamental crown was approximately 75 lbs (34 kg). See P. K. McCarter, II Samuel (AB), 313.
n100132:
tn Heb вҖңAmnon the son of David loved her.вҖқ The following verse indicates the extreme nature of his infatuation, so the translation uses вҖңmadly in loveвҖқ here.
sn Amnon was the half-brother of Tamar; Absalom was her full blood-brother.
n100133: tn Heb вҖңand there was distress to Amnon so that he made himself sick.вҖқ
n100135: tn A more idiomatic translation might be вҖңWhy are you of all peopleвҖҰ?вҖқ
n100136: tn This verb is used in the Hitpael stem only in this chapter of the Hebrew Bible. With the exception of v. 2 it describes not a real sickness but one pretended in order to entrap Tamar. The Hitpael sometimes, as here, describes the subject making oneself appear to be of a certain character. On this use of the stem, see GKC 149-50 В§54.e.
n1001310: tc A few medieval Hebrew mss supported by the LXX and Vulgate read the Hiphil вҖҸЧ•Ц·ЧҷЦјЧ•Ц№ЧҰЦҙЧҷЧҗЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (vayyotsiКҫu) вҖңand they removed everyone,вҖқ rather than the MTвҖҷs Qal вҖҸЧ•Ц·ЧҷЦјЦөЧҰЦ°ЧҗЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (vayyetseКҫu, вҖңthey leftвҖқ). This verb would then match the instructions more closely.
n1001311: tn Heb вҖңlie with meвҖқ (so NAB, NASB, NRSV); NCV вҖңcome and have sexual relations with me.вҖқ
n1001312: tn Heb вҖңand you will be like one of the fools.вҖқ
n1001315: tn Heb вҖңand he humiliated her and lay with her.вҖқ
n1001316: tn Heb вҖңand Amnon hated her with very great hatred.вҖқ
n1001317: tn Heb вҖңfor greater was the hatred with which he hated her than the love with which he loved her.вҖқ
n1001318: tn Heb вҖңNo, because this great evil is [worse] than the other which you did with me, by sending me away.вҖқ Perhaps the broken syntax reflects her hysteria and outrage.
n1001319: tn Heb вҖңsend this [one] from upon me to the outside.вҖқ
n1001320: tn The Hebrew expression used here (вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ°ЧӘЦ№Ч Ц¶ЧӘ ЧӨЦјЦ·ЧЎЦјЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ, ketonet passim) is found only here and in Gen 37:3, 23, 32. Hebrew вҖҸЧӨЦјЦ·ЧЎвҖҺ (pas) can refer to the palm of the hand or the sole of the foot; here the idea is probably that of a long robe reaching to the feet and having sleeves reaching to the wrists. The notion of a вҖңcoat of many colorsвҖқ (KJV, ASV вҖңgarment of divers colorsвҖқ), a familiar translation for the phrase in Genesis, is based primarily on the translation adopted in the LXX ПҮО№П„бҝ¶ОҪОұ ПҖОҝО№ОәОҜО»ОҝОҪ (chitЕҚna poikilon) and does not have a great deal of support.
n1001321: tn Heb вҖңhisвҖқ; the referent (Amnon) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1001322: tn The Hebrew verb is a perfect with nonconsecutive vav, probably indicating an action (locking the door) that complements the preceding one (pushing her out the door).
n1001323: tn Heb вҖңDonвҖҷt set your heart to this thing!вҖқ Elsewhere this phrase means to disregard or not pay attention to something (e.g. Exod 7:23). It is a callous thing to say to Tamar, but to the degree that what he said becomes known, it misleads people from understanding that he is personally plotting revenge (13:22, 28).
n1001324: tc The LXX and part of the Old Latin tradition include the following addition to v. 21, also included in some English versions (e.g., NAB, NRSV, CEV): вҖңBut he did not grieve the spirit of Amnon his son, because he loved him, since he was his firstborn.вҖқ Note DavidвҖҷs attitude toward his son Adonijah in 1 Kgs 1:6.
n1001326: tn Heb вҖңyour servant has sheepshearers.вҖқ The phrase вҖңyour servantвҖқ also occurs at the end of the verse and is translated as "me".
n1001327: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (Absalom) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1001328: tc Here and in v. 27 the translation follows 4QSama вҖҸЧ•ЧҷЧӨЧҰЧЁвҖҺ (vayyiftsar, вҖңand he pressedвҖқ) rather than the MT вҖҸЧ•Ц·ЧҷЦјЦҙЧӨЦ°ЧЁЦёЧҘвҖҺ (vayyifrats, вҖңand he broke throughвҖқ). This emended reading seems also to underlie the translations of the LXX (ОәОұбҪ¶ бјҗОІО№О¬ПғОұП„Оҝ, kai ebiasato), the Syriac Peshitta (weКҫalseh), and Vulgate (cogeret eum).
n1001329: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (the king) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1001330: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (David) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1001332: tn Heb вҖңwhen good is the heart of Amnon with wine.вҖқ
n1001334: tn Heb вҖңit was placed on the mouth of Absalom.вҖқ
n1001335: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (Amnon) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1001337: tn Heb вҖңand they lifted their voice and wept.вҖқ
n1001339: tc The Hebrew text leaves the word вҖңDavidвҖқ to be inferred. The Syriac Peshitta and Vulgate add the word вҖңDavid.вҖқ Most of the Greek tradition includes the words вҖңKing DavidвҖқ here.
n1001340: tc The translation follows 4QSama in reading вҖҸЧЁЧ•ЦјЧ—Ц· Ч”Ц·ЧһЦјЦ¶ЧңЦ¶ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ (ruakh hammelekh, вҖңthe spirit of the kingвҖқ) rather than the MT вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦёЧ•ЦҙЧ“ Ч”Ц·ЧһЦјЦ¶ЧңЦ¶ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ (david hammelekh, вҖңDavid the kingвҖқ). The understanding reflected in the translation above is that David, though alienated during this time from his son Absalom, still had an abiding love and concern for him. He longed for reconciliation with him. A rather different interpretation of the verse supposes that DavidвҖҷs interest in taking military action against Absalom grew slack with the passing of time, and this in turn enabled DavidвҖҷs advisers to encourage him toward reconciliation with Absalom. For the latter view, see P. K. McCarter, II Samuel (AB), 344, and cf. CEV.
n1001341: tn Heb вҖңwas consoled over Amnon, because he was dead.вҖқ
n100141: tn Heb вҖңthe heart of the king was upon.вҖқ The Syriac Peshitta adds the verb КҫethreКҝi (вҖңwas reconciledвҖқ).
n100142: tn The Hebrew Hitpael verbal form here indicates pretended rather than genuine action.
n100144: tn Heb вҖңput the words in her mouthвҖқ (so NASB, NIV).
n100145: tc The translation follows many medieval Hebrew mss in reading вҖҸЧ•Ц·ЧӘЦјЦ·Ч‘Ц№ЧҗвҖҺ (vattavoКҫ, вҖңand she wentвҖқ) rather than the MT вҖҸЧ•Ц·ЧӘЦјЦ№ЧҗЧһЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ (vattoКҫmer, вҖңand she saidвҖқ). The MT reading shows confusion with вҖҸЧ•Ц·ЧӘЦјЦ№ЧҗЧһЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ later in the verse. The emendation suggested here is supported by the LXX, the Syriac Peshitta, some mss of the Targum, and Vulgate.
n100146: tn The word вҖңmeвҖқ is left to be inferred in the Hebrew text; it is present in the Syriac Peshitta and Vulgate.
n100148: tn Here and elsewhere (vv. 7, 12, 15a, 17, 19) the woman uses a term which suggests a lower level female servant. She uses the term to express her humility before the king. However, she uses a different term in vv. 15b-16. See the note at v. 15 for a discussion of the rhetorical purpose of this switch in terminology.
n100149: tn Heb вҖңin exchange for the life.вҖқ The Hebrew preposition вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ (bet, вҖңinвҖқ) here is the so-called bet pretii, or bet (вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ) of price, defining the value attached to someone or something.
n1001410: sn My remaining coal is here metaphorical language, describing the one remaining son as her only source of lingering hope for continuing the family line.
n1001412: tn The words вҖңin that caseвҖқ are not in the Hebrew text, but may be inferred from the context. They are supplied in the translation for the sake of clarification.
n1001415: tn Heb вҖңhe devises plans for the one banished from him not to be banished.вҖқ
n1001416: tc The LXX (бҪ„ПҲОөП„ОұОҜ ОјОө, opsetai me) has misunderstood the Hebrew вҖҸЧҷЦөЧЁЦ°ЧҗЦ»Ч ЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (yereКҫuni, Piel perfect, вҖңthey have made me fearfulвҖқ), taking the verb to be a form of the verb вҖҸЧЁЦёЧҗЦёЧ”вҖҺ (raКҫah, вҖңto seeвҖқ) rather than the verb вҖҸЧҷЦёЧЁЦөЧҗвҖҺ (yareКҫ, вҖңto fearвҖқ). The fact that the Greek translators were working with an unvocalized Hebrew text (i.e., consonants only) made them very susceptible to this type of error.
n1001417: tn Here and in v. 16 the woman refers to herself as the kingвҖҷs вҖҸЧҗЦёЧһЦёЧ”вҖҺ (Кҫamah), a term that refers to a higher level female servant toward whom the master might have some obligation. Like the other term, this word expresses her humility, but it also suggests that the king might have some obligation to treat her in accordance with the principles of justice.
n1001419: tn Or вҖңwill.вҖқ The imperfect verbal form can have either an indicative or modal nuance. The use of вҖңperhapsвҖқ in v. 15b suggests the latter here.
n1001420: tn Heb вҖңin order to deliver his maid.вҖқ
n1001422: tn Heb вҖңfrom the inheritance of God.вҖқ The expression refers to the property that was granted to her family line in the division of the land authorized by God.
n1001423: tn Heb вҖңIs the hand of Joab with you in all this?вҖқ
n1001424: tn Heb вҖңto know all that is in the land.вҖқ

n1001427: tc The present translation reads with the Qere вҖңyourвҖқ rather than the MT вҖңhis.вҖқ
n1001431: tn Heb вҖңLike Absalom there was not a handsome man in all Israel to boast exceedingly.вҖқ

n1001510: tc The MT has here вҖңforty,вҖқ but this is presumably a scribal error for вҖңfour.вҖқ The context will not tolerate a period of forty years prior to the rebellion of Absalom. The Lucianic Greek recension (П„ОӯПғПғОұПҒОұ бј”П„О·, tessara etД“), the Syriac Peshitta (КҫarbaКҝ sanin), and Vulgate (post quattuor autem annos) in fact have the expected reading вҖңfour years.вҖқ Most English translations follow the versions in reading вҖңfourвҖқ here, although some (e.g. KJV, ASV, NASB, NKJV), following the MT, read вҖңforty.вҖқ
n1001511: tn Heb вҖңfor your servant vowed a vow.вҖқ The formal court style of referring to oneвҖҷs self in third person (вҖңyour servantвҖқ) has been translated here as first person for clarity.
n1001513: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (Absalom) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1001515: tn Heb вҖңbeing invited and going naively and they did not know anything.вҖқ
n1001516: tn Traditionally, вҖңcounselor,вҖқ but this term is more often associated with psychological counseling today, so вҖңadviserвҖқ was used in the translation instead.
n1001517: tn Heb вҖңAbsalom sent for Ahithophel the Gilonite, the adviser of David, from his city, from Giloh, while he was sacrificing.вҖқ It is not entirely clear who (Absalom or Ahithophel) was offering the sacrifices.

n1001530: tn Heb вҖңcrossing over near the face of.вҖқ

n1001534: tn Heb вҖңloyal love and truth.вҖқ The expression is a hendiadys.
n1001545: tn The Greek tradition understands the Hebrew word as an imperative (вҖңseeвҖқ). Most Greek mss have бјҙОҙОөП„Оө (idete); the Lucianic recension has ОІО»ОӯПҖОө (blepe). It could just as well be taken as a question: вҖңDonвҖҷt you see what is happening?вҖқ The present translation takes the word as a question, with the implication that Zadok is a priest and not a prophet (i.e., вҖңseerвҖқ) and therefore unable to know what the future holds.
n1001546: tn Heb вҖңAnd Ahimaaz your son, and Jonathan the son of Abiathar, two of your sons, with you.вҖқ The pronominal suffix on the last word is plural, referring to Zadok and Abiathar.
n1001547: tn The pronoun is plural, referring to Zadok and Abiathar.
n1001548: tc The translation follows 4QSama, part of the Greek tradition, the Syriac Peshitta, Targum, and Vulgate in reading вҖңand to David,вҖқ rather than MT вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч“ЦёЧ•ЦҙЧ“вҖҺ (vedavid, вҖңand DavidвҖқ). As Driver points out, the Hebrew verb вҖҸЧ”ЦҙЧ’ЦјЦҙЧҷЧ“вҖҺ (higgid, вҖңhe relatedвҖқ) never uses the accusative for the person to whom something is told (S. R. Driver, Notes on the Hebrew Text and the Topography of the Books of Samuel, 316).
n1001551: tn Heb вҖңWill not Zadok and Abiathar the priests be there with you?вҖқ The rhetorical question draws attention to the fact that Hushai will not be alone.
n1001552: tn Heb вҖңfrom the house of the king.вҖқ
n1001553: tn Heb вҖңand you must send by their hand to me every word which you hear.вҖқ Both of the second person verb forms are plural with Zadok, Abiathar, and Hushai being the understood subjects.
n100163: tc The translation follows the Qere and many medieval Hebrew mss in reading вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч”Ц·ЧңЦјЦ¶Ч—Ц¶ЧқвҖҺ (vehallekhem, вҖңand the breadвҖқ) rather than вҖҸЧ•ЦјЧңЦ°Ч”Ц·ЧңЦјЦ¶Ч—Ц¶ЧқвҖҺ (ulehallekhem, вҖңand to the breadвҖқ) of the Kethib. The syntax of the MT is confused here by the needless repetition of the preposition, probably taken from the preceding word.
n100164: tn The Hebrew text adds вҖңto drink.вҖқ
n100168: tn Heb вҖңcame to.вҖқ The form of the verb in the MT is odd. Some prefer to read вҖҸЧ•Ц·ЧҷЦјЦ·Ч‘Ц№ЧҗвҖҺ (vayyavoКҫ, preterite with vav consecutive) rather than вҖҸЧ•ЦјЧ‘ЦёЧҗвҖҺ (uvaКҫ, apparently perfect with vav), but this is probably an instance where the narrative offline veqatal construction introduces a new scene.
n100169: tn Heb вҖңAnd look, from there a man was coming out from the clan of the house of Saul and his name was Shimei son of Gera, continually going out and cursing.вҖқ
n1001612: tn Heb вҖңWhat to me and to you?вҖқ
n1001613: tn Heb вҖңwho came out from my entrails.вҖқ DavidвҖҷs point is that is his own son, his child whom he himself had fathered, was now wanting to kill him.
n1001614: tc The Hebrew text is difficult here. It is probably preferable to read with the LXX, the Syriac Peshitta, and Vulgate вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°ЧўЧ•Ц№Ч ЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (beКҝonyi, вҖңon my afflictionвҖқ) rather than the Kethib of the MT вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧўЦ·Ч•Ц№Ч ЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (baКҝavoni, вҖңon my wrongdoingвҖқ). While this Kethib reading is understandable as an objective genitive (i.e., вҖңthe wrong perpetrated upon meвҖқ), it does not conform to normal Hebrew idiom for this idea. The Qere of the MT вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°ЧўЦөЧҷЧ ЦөЧҷвҖҺ (beКҝeni, вҖңon my eyesвҖқ), usually taken as synecdoche to mean вҖңmy tears,вҖқ does not commend itself as a likely meaning. The Hebrew word is one of the so-called tiqqune sopherim, or вҖңemendations of the scribes.вҖқ
n1001615: tn Heb вҖңand the Lord will restore to me good in place of his curse this day.вҖқ
n1001616: tn Heb вҖңand he cursed and threw stones, opposite him, pelting [them] with dirt.вҖқ The offline veqatal construction in the last clause indicates an action that was complementary to the action described in the preceding clause. He simultaneously threw stones and dirt.
n1001617: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (David) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1001618: tn Heb вҖңand all the people, the men of Israel.вҖқ

n1001621: tn Heb вҖңNo for with the one whom the Lord has chosen, and this people, and all the men of Israel, I will be and with him I will stay.вҖқ The translation follows the Qere and several medieval Hebrew mss in reading вҖҸЧңЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (lo, вҖң[I will be] to himвҖқ) rather than the MT вҖҸЧңЦ№ЧҗвҖҺ (loКҫ, вҖң[I will] not beвҖқ), which makes very little sense here.
n1001622: tn Heb вҖңJust as I served before your father, so I will be before you.вҖқ
n1001623: tn Heb вҖңapproach,вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЧ•Ц№ЧҗвҖҺ (boКҫ) with the preposition вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧңвҖҺ (Кҫel) means вҖңcome toвҖқ or вҖңapproach,вҖқ but is also used as a euphemism for sexual relations.
n1001624: tn Heb вҖңand the hands of all who are with you will be strengthened.вҖқ

n100173: tc Heb вҖңlike the returning of all, the man whom you are seeking.вҖқ The LXX reads differently: вҖңAnd I will return all the people to you the way a bride returns to her husband, except for the life of the one man whom you are seeking.вҖқ The other early versions also struggled with this verse. Modern translations are divided as well: the NAB, NRSV, REB, and NLT follow the LXX, while the NASB and NIV follow the Hebrew text.
n100174: tn Heb вҖңall of the people will be safe.вҖқ

n1001710: tn Heb вҖңthat he falls on them [i.e., AbsalomвҖҷs troops] at the first [encounter]; or вҖңthat some of them [i.e., AbsalomвҖҷs troops] fall at the first [encounter].вҖқ
n1001713: tn Heb вҖңsend quickly and tell David saying.вҖқ
n1001714: tc The MT reads вҖңthe rift valleys (вҖҸЧўЦІЧЁЦёЧ‘Ч•Ц№ЧӘвҖҺ, Кҝaravot) of the wilderness.вҖқ The plural form typically refers to the gently sloping plains at the basin of the rift valley just north of the Dead Sea (while the larger rift valley extends from Galilee to the Gulf of Aqaba). Many translations render as the вҖңfordsвҖқ (NASB, ESV, NIV, NRSV) assuming the reversal of two letters as вҖҸЧўЦІЧ‘ЦёЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧӘвҖҺ (Кҝavarot, вҖңfords, crossingвҖқ).
n1001715: tn That is, вҖңcross over the Jordan River.вҖқ
n1001717: tn Heb вҖңtheyвҖқ; the referents (AbsalomвҖҷs men) have been specified in the translation for clarity.

n1001721: tn Heb вҖңfor thus Ahithophel has devised against you.вҖқ The expression вҖңthusвҖқ is narrative shorthand, referring to the plan outlined by Ahithophel (see vv. 1-3). The men would surely have outlined the plan in as much detail as they had been given by the messenger.
n1001722: tn The word вҖңRiverвҖқ is not in the Hebrew text here or in v. 24, but has been supplied in the translation for clarity.

n1001726: tc The MT adds вҖңroasted grainвҖқ вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч§ЦёЧңЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (veqali) at the end of v. 28, apparently accidentally repeating the word from its earlier occurrence in this verse. With the LXX, the Syriac Peshitta, and an Old Latin ms the translation deletes this second occurrence of the word.

n1001728: tn Or вҖңwildernessвҖқ (so KJV, NASB, NRSV, TEV, NLT).

n100184: tc The translation follows the LXX (except for the Lucianic recension), Symmachus, and Vulgate in reading вҖҸЧҗЦёЧӘЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ (Кҫattah, вҖңyouвҖқ) rather than MT вҖҸЧўЦёЧӘЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ (Кҝattah, вҖңnowвҖқ).

n1001813: tc The translation follows the Qere and many medieval Hebrew mss in reading вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧңЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (velu, вҖңand ifвҖқ) rather than MT вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧңЦ№ЧҗвҖҺ (veloвҖҷ, вҖңand notвҖқ).

n1001818: tc The Hebrew text is very difficult here. The MT reads вҖҸЧһЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (mi, вҖңwhoвҖқ), apparently yielding the following sense: вҖңShow care, whoever you might be, for the youth Absalom.вҖқ The Syriac Peshitta reads li (вҖңfor meвҖқ), the Hebrew counterpart of which may also lie behind the LXX rendering ОјОҝО№ (moi, вҖңfor meвҖқ). This reading seems preferable here, since it restores sense to the passage and most easily explains the rise of the variant.
n1001819: tc The translation follows the Qere, many medieval Hebrew mss, and a number of the ancient versions in reading вҖҸЧ‘Ц°Ч Ц·ЧӨЦ°Ч©ЧҒЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (venafshi, вҖңagainst my lifeвҖқ) rather than the MT вҖҸЧ‘Ц°Ч Ц·ЧӨЦ°Ч©ЧҒЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (venafsho, вҖңagainst his lifeвҖқ).
n1001821: tn There is a play on the word вҖңheartвҖқ here that is difficult to reproduce in English. Literally the Hebrew text says вҖңhe took three spears in his hand and thrust them into the heart of Absalom while he was still alive in the heart of the oak tree.вҖқ This figure of speech involves the use of the same word in different senses and is known as antanaclasis. It is illustrated in the familiar saying from the time of the American Revolution: вҖңIf we donвҖҷt hang together, we will all hang separately.вҖқ The present translation understands вҖңheartвҖқ to be used somewhat figuratively for вҖңchestвҖқ (cf. TEV, CEV), which explains why JoabвҖҷs armor-bearers could still вҖңkillвҖқ Absalom after he had been stabbed with three spears through the вҖңheart.вҖқ Since trees do not have вҖңchestsвҖқ either, the translation uses вҖңmiddle.вҖқ
n1001823: tn Heb вҖңand all Israel fled, each to his tent.вҖқ In this context this refers to the supporters of Absalom (see vv. 6-7, 16).
n1001825: tn This disjunctive clause (conjunction + subject + verb) describes an occurrence that preceded the events just narrated.

n1001841: sn This marks the beginning of ch. 19 in the Hebrew text. Beginning with 18:33, the verse numbers through 19:43 in the English Bible differ from the verse numbers in the Hebrew text (BHS), with 18:33 ET = 19:1 HT, 19:1 ET = 19:2 HT, 19:2 ET = 19:3 HT, etc., through 19:43 ET = 19:44 HT. From 20:1 the versification in the English Bible and the Hebrew Bible is again the same.

n1001920: tn Heb вҖңthe king.вҖқ The pronoun (вҖңhimвҖқ) has been used in the translation to avoid redundancy.
n1001923: tn Though this verb in the MT is third person masculine singular, it should probably be read as second person masculine singular. It is one of fifteen places where the Masoretes placed a dot over each of the letters of the word in question in order to call attention to their suspicion of the word. Their concern in this case apparently had to do with the fact that this verb and the two preceding verbs alternate from third person to second and back again to third. Words marked in this way in Hebrew manuscripts or printed editions are said to have puncta extrordinaria, or вҖңextraordinary points.вҖқ
n1001925: tn The Hebrew text has simply вҖңyour servant.вҖқ The word "I" has been supplied for English style.
