n1001935: tn Heb вҖңand heвҖқ; the referent (the servant) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1001938: tn Heb вҖңand you placed your servant among those who eat at your table.вҖқ
n1001942: tc The MT reading вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧӘЦҫЧ‘Ц·ЧҷЦјЦ·ЧЁЦ°Ч“ЦјЦөЧҹвҖҺ (Кҫet vayyarden, вҖңin the JordanвҖқ) is odd syntactically. The use of the preposition after the object marker вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ (Кҫet) is difficult to explain. Graphic confusion is likely in the MT; the translation assumes the reading вҖҸЧһЦҙЧҷЦјЦ·ЧЁЦ°Ч“ЦјЦөЧҹвҖҺ (miyyarden, вҖңfrom the JordanвҖқ). Another possibility is to read the definite article on the front of вҖңJordanвҖқ (вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧҷЦјЦ·ЧЁЦ°Ч“ЦјЦөЧҹвҖҺ, hayyarden; вҖңthe JordanвҖқ).
n1001946: tn Heb вҖңLike a little your servant will cross the Jordan with the king.вҖқ
n1001949: tn The MT in this instance alone spells the name with final вҖҸЧҹвҖҺ (nun, вҖңKimhanвҖқ) rather than as elsewhere with final вҖҸЧқвҖҺ (mem, вҖңKimhamвҖқ). As in most other translations, the conventional spelling (with вҖҸЧқвҖҺ) has been used here to avoid confusion.
n1001951: tc The translation follows the Qere and many medieval Hebrew mss in reading the Hiphil verb вҖҸЧ”Ц¶ЧўЦұЧ‘ЦҙЧҷЧЁЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (heКҝeviru, вҖңthey caused to pass overвҖқ) rather than the Qal verb вҖҸЧ•Ц·ЧҷЦ°ЧўЦұЧ‘ЦҙЧЁЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (vayeКҝeviru, вҖңthey crossed overвҖқ) of the MT.
n1001954: tn The translation understands the verb in a desiderative sense, indicating the desire but not necessarily the completed action of the party in question. It is possible, however, that the verb should be given the more common sense of accomplished action, in which case it means here вҖңWhy have you cursed us?вҖқ
n100202: tn The expression used here вҖҸЧҷЦ°ЧһЦҙЧҷЧ ЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (yemini) is a short form of the more common вҖңBenjamin.вҖқ It appears elsewhere in 1 Sam 9:4 and Esth 2:5. Cf. 1 Sam 9:1.
n100203: tn Heb вҖңthe shofarвҖқ (the ramвҖҷs horn trumpet). So also v. 22.
n100204: tc The MT reads вҖҸЧңЦ°ЧҗЦ№Ч”ЦёЧңЦёЧҷЧ•вҖҺ (leКҫohalayv, вҖңto his tentsвҖқ). For a similar idiom, see 19:9. An ancient scribal tradition understands the reading to be вҖҸЧңЦөЧҗЧңЦ№Ч”ЦёЧҷЧ•вҖҺ (leКҫlohav, вҖңto his godsвҖқ). The word is a tiqqun sopherim, and the scribes indicate that they changed the word from вҖңgodsвҖқ to вҖңtentsвҖқ so as to soften its theological implications. In a consonantal Hebrew text the change involved only the metathesis of two letters.
n100206: tn The word вҖңRiverвҖқ is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied in the translation for clarity.
n100209: tn Heb вҖңand he placed them in a guarded house.вҖқ
n1002010: tn Heb вҖңcome to them.вҖқ The expression вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЧ•Ц№Чҗ ЧҗЦ¶ЧңвҖҺ (boКҫ Кҫel) means вҖңcome toвҖқ or вҖңapproach,вҖқ but is also used as a euphemism for sexual relations.
n1002011: tn The present translation follows the Masoretic accentuation, with the major mark of disjunction (i.e., the вҖҷatnakh) placed at the word вҖңdays.вҖқ However, some scholars have suggested moving the вҖҷatnakh to вҖңJudahвҖқ a couple of words earlier. This would yield the following sense: вҖңThree days, and you be present here with them.вҖқ The difference in meaning is slight, and the MT is acceptable as it stands.
n1002012: tn Heb вҖңfind.вҖқ The perfect verbal form is unexpected with the preceding word вҖңotherwise.вҖқ We should probably read instead the imperfect. Although it is possible to understand the perfect here as indicating that the feared result is thought of as already having taken place (cf. BDB 814 s.v. вҖҸЧӨЦјЦ¶ЧҹвҖҺ 2), it is more likely that the perfect is simply the result of scribal error. In this context the imperfect would be more consistent with the following verb вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч”ЦҙЧҰЦјЦҙЧҷЧңвҖҺ (vehitsil, вҖңand he will get awayвҖқ).
n1002013: sn The significance of the statement it fell out here is unclear. If the dagger fell out of its sheath before Joab got to Amasa, how then did he kill him? Josephus, Ant. 7.11.7 (7.284), suggested that as Joab approached Amasa he deliberately caused the dagger to fall to the ground at an opportune moment as though by accident. When he bent over and picked it up, he then stabbed Amasa with it. Others have tried to make a case for thinking that two swords are referred toвҖ”the one that fell out and another that Joab kept concealed until the last moment. But nothing in the text clearly supports this view. Perhaps JosephusвҖҷ understanding is best, but it is by no means obvious in the text either.
n1002014: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (Joab) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1002015: tn Heb вҖңhisвҖқ; the referent (Amasa) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1002016: tn Heb вҖңand he did not repeat concerning him, and he died.вҖқ
n1002018: tn Heb вҖңhimвҖқ; the referent (Amasa) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1002019: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (the man who spoke up in v. 11) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1002020: tn Heb вҖңAmasa.вҖқ For stylistic reasons the name has been replaced by the pronoun (вҖңhimвҖқ) in the translation.
n1002021: tn Heb вҖңhimвҖқ; the referent (Amasa) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1002022: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (Sheba) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1002023: tc In keeping with the form of the name in v. 15, the translation deletes the вҖңandвҖқ found in the MT.
n1002024: tc The translation follows the Qere, many medieval Hebrew mss, and the ancient versions in reading вҖҸЧ•Ц·ЧҷЦјЦҙЧ§ЦјЦёЧ”ЦІЧңЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (vayyiqqahalu, вҖңand they were gathered togetherвҖқ) rather than the Kethib of the MT вҖҸЧ•Ц·ЧҷЦјЦҙЧ§Ц°ЧңЦ»Ч”Ч•ЦјвҖҺ (vayyiqluhu, вҖңand they cursed himвҖқ). The Kethib is the result of metathesis.
n1002025: tn Heb вҖңthey.вҖқ The following context makes it clear that this refers to Joab and his army.
n1002026: tc The LXX has here бјҗОҪОҝОҝбҝҰПғОұОҪ (enoousan, вҖңwere devisingвҖқ), which apparently presupposes the Hebrew word вҖҸЧһЦ·Ч—ЦІЧ©ЦёЧҒЧ‘ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (makhashavim) rather than the MT вҖҸЧһЦ·Ч©ЧҒЦ°Ч—ЦҙЧҷЧӘЦҙЧқвҖҺ (mashkhitim, вҖңwere destroyingвҖқ). With a number of other scholars Driver thinks that the Greek variant may preserve the original reading, but this seems to be an unnecessary conclusion (but see S. R. Driver, Notes on the Hebrew Text and the Topography of the Books of Samuel, 346).
n1002027: tn Heb вҖңa city and a mother.вҖқ The expression is a hendiadys, meaning that this city was an important one in Israel and had smaller cities dependent on it.
n1002028: tn Heb вҖңFar be it, far be it from me.вҖқ The expression is clearly emphatic, as may be seen in part by the repetition. P. K. McCarter, however, understands it to be coarser than the translation adopted here. He renders it as вҖңIвҖҷll be damned ifвҖҰвҖқ (II Samuel [AB], 426, 429), which (while it is not a literal translation) may not be too far removed from the way a soldier might have expressed himself.
n1002031: tn Heb вҖңthe head of Sheba son of Bikri.вҖқ
n1002032: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (Joab) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1002033: tn Heb вҖңtheyвҖқ; the referent (JoabвҖҷs men) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1002035: tn Heb вҖңAdoramвҖқ (so KJV, NAB, NRSV, CEV), but see 1 Kgs 4:6; 5:14.
n1002037: tn Heb вҖңpriest for David.вҖқ KJV (вҖңa chief ruler about DavidвҖқ) and ASV (вҖңchief minister unto DavidвҖқ) regarded this office as political.
n100211: tn Heb вҖңsought the face of the Lord.вҖқ
n100214: tn After the preceding imperfect verbal form, the subordinated imperative indicates purpose/result. S. R. Driver comments, вҖңвҖҰthe imper. is used instead of the more normal voluntative, for the purpose of expressing with somewhat greater force the intention of the previous verbвҖқ (S. R. Driver, Notes on the Hebrew Text and the Topography of the Books of Samuel, 350).
n100215: tc The translation follows the Qere and several medieval Hebrew mss in reading вҖҸЧңЦёЧ Ч•ЦјвҖҺ (lanu, вҖңto usвҖқ) rather than the MT вҖҸЧңЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (li, вҖңto meвҖқ). But for a contrary opinion see S. R. Driver, Notes on the Hebrew Text and the Topography of the Books of Samuel, 53, 350.
n100217: tn Heb вҖңand he saidвҖқ; the referent (David) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n100218: tn The exact nature of this execution is not altogether clear. The verb вҖҸЧҷЦёЧ§Ц·ЧўвҖҺ (yaqaКҝ) basically means вҖңto dislocateвҖқ or вҖңalienate.вҖқ In Gen 32:26 it is used of the dislocation of JacobвҖҷs thigh. Figuratively it can refer to the removal of an individual from a group (e.g., Jer 6:8; Ezek 23:17) or to a type of punishment the specific identity of which is uncertain (e.g., here and Num 25:4); cf. NAB вҖңdismember themвҖқ; NIV вҖңto be killed and exposed.вҖқ
n100219: tc The LXX reads вҖңat Gibeon on the mountain of the LordвҖқ (cf. 21:9). The present translation follows the MT, although a number of recent English translations follow the LXX reading here (e.g., NAB, NRSV, NLT).
n1002110: tc The MT reads вҖңMichalвҖқ here, but two Hebrew manuscripts read вҖңMerab,вҖқ along with some LXX manuscripts. Cf. 1 Sam 18:19.
n1002111: tc The translation follows the Qere and several medieval Hebrew mss in reading вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ°Ч‘Ц·ЧўЦ°ЧӘЦјЦёЧқвҖҺ (shevaКҝtam, вҖңthe seven of themвҖқ) rather than MT вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧ‘Ц·ЧўЦ°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (shivaКҝtim, вҖңseventyвҖқ).
n1002113: tc The translation follows the Qere and many medieval Hebrew mss in reading вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦҙЧӘЦ°Ч—ЦҙЧңЦјЦ·ЧӘвҖҺ (bitkhillat, вҖңin the beginningвҖқ) rather than MT вҖҸЧӘЦ°Ч—ЦҙЧңЦјЦ·ЧӘвҖҺ (tekhillat, вҖңbeginning ofвҖқ).
n1002114: tn Heb вҖңuntil water was poured on them from the sky.вҖқ
n1002117: tn Heb вҖңDavid.вҖқ For stylistic reasons the name has been replaced by the pronoun (вҖңheвҖқ) in the translation.
n1002118: tn Heb вҖңthe bones of Saul and the bones of Jonathan his son.вҖқ See also v. 13.
n1002121: tc Against the MT, this word is better read without the definite article. The MT reading is probably here the result of wrong word division, with the letter вҖҸЧ”вҖҺ (he) belonging with the preceding word вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧқвҖҺ (sham) as the he directive (i.e., вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧһЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ, samah, вҖңto thereвҖқ).
n1002125: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (David) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1002126: tc Many medieval Hebrew mss have here вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ°ЧӣЦ№ЧңвҖҺ (kekhol, вҖңaccording to allвҖқ).
n1002127: tn Heb вҖңwas entreated.вҖқ The verb is an example of the so-called niphal tolerativum, with the sense that God allowed himself to be supplicated through prayer (cf. GKC 137 В§51.c).
n1002129: tn This name has the definite article and may be intended to refer to a group of people rather than a single individual with this name.
n1002130: tn This is the only occurrence of this Hebrew word in the OT. Its precise meaning is therefore somewhat uncertain. As early as the LXX the word was understood to refer to a вҖңspear,вҖқ and this seems to be the most likely possibility. Some scholars have proposed emending the text of 2 Sam 21:16 to вҖҸЧӣЧ•Ц№Ч‘Ц·ЧўЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (khovaКҝo; вҖңhis helmetвҖқ), but in spite of the fact that the word вҖңhelmetвҖқ appears in 1 Sam 17:5, there is not much evidence for reading that word here.
n1002131:
tn Either the word вҖңshekelsвҖқ should be supplied here, or the Hebrew word вҖҸЧһЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦ°Ч§Ц·ЧңвҖҺ (mishqal, вҖңweight ofвҖқ) right before вҖңbronzeвҖқ is a corrupted form of the word for shekel. If the latter is the case the problem probably resulted from another occurrence of the word вҖҸЧһЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦ°Ч§Ц·ЧңвҖҺ just four words earlier in the verse.
sn 300 bronze shekels would have weighed about 7.5 pounds (3.4 kg).
n1002132: tn The Hebrew text reads simply вҖңa new [thing],вҖқ prompting one to ask вҖңA new what?вҖқ Several possibilities have been proposed to resolve the problem: perhaps a word has dropped out of the Hebrew text here; or perhaps the word вҖңnewвҖқ is the result of misreading a different, less common, word; or perhaps a word (e.g., вҖңsword,вҖқ so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, CEV, NLT) is simply to be inferred. The translation generally follows the last possibility, while at the same time being deliberately nonspecific (вҖңweaponвҖқ).
n1002133: tn Heb вҖңJaare-Oregim,вҖқ but the second word, which means вҖңweavers,вҖқ is probably accidentally included. It appears at the end of the verse. The term is omitted in the parallel account in 1 Chr 20:5, which has simply вҖңJair.вҖқ
n1002134: sn The Hebrew text as it stands reads, вҖңElhanan son of Jaare-Oregim the Bethlehemite killed Goliath the Gittite.вҖқ Who killed Goliath the Gittite? According to 1 Sam 17:4-58 it was David who killed Goliath, but according to the MT of 2 Sam 21:19 it was Elhanan who killed him. Many scholars believe that the two passages are hopelessly at variance with one another. Others have proposed various solutions to the difficulty, such as identifying David with Elhanan or positing the existence of two Goliaths. But in all likelihood the problem is the result of difficulties in the textual transmission of the Samuel passage. The parallel passage in 1 Chr 20:5 reads, вҖңElhanan son of Jair killed Lahmi the brother of Goliath.вҖқ Both versions are textually suspect. The Chronicles text appears to have misread вҖңBethlehemiteвҖқ (вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦөЧҷЧӘ Ч”Ц·ЧңЦјЦ·Ч—Ц°ЧһЦҙЧҷвҖҺ, bet hallakhmi) as the accusative sign followed by a proper name вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧӘ ЧңЦ·Ч—Ц°ЧһЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (Кҫet lakhmi). (See the note at 1 Chr 20:5.) The Samuel text appears to have misread the word for вҖңbrotherвҖқ (вҖҸЧҗЦ·Ч—вҖҺ, Кҫakh) as the accusative sign (вҖҸЧҗЦөЧӘвҖҺ, Кҫet), thereby giving the impression that Elhanan, not David, killed Goliath. Thus in all probability the original text read, вҖңElhanan son of Jair the Bethlehemite killed the brother of Goliath.вҖқ
n100221: sn In this long song of thanks, David affirms that God is his faithful protector. He recalls in highly poetic fashion how God intervened in awesome power and delivered him from death. His experience demonstrates that God vindicates those who are blameless and remain loyal to him. True to his promises, God gives the king victory on the battlefield and enables him to subdue nations. A parallel version of the song appears in Ps 18.
n100223: tn Heb вҖңin the day,вҖқ or вҖңat the time.вҖқ
n100225: tn Heb вҖңand from the hand of Saul.вҖқ
n100226: tn Traditionally вҖңis my rockвҖқ; CEV вҖңmighty rockвҖқ; TEV вҖңis my protector.вҖқ This metaphor pictures God as a rocky, relatively inaccessible summit, where one would be able to find protection from enemies. See 1 Sam 23:25, 28.
n100227:
tn Traditionally вҖңmy fortressвҖқ; TEV вҖңmy strong fortressвҖқ; NCV вҖңmy protection.вҖқ
sn My stronghold. David often found safety in such strongholds. See 1 Sam 22:4-5; 24:22; 2 Sam 5:9, 17; 23:14.
n100228: tc The translation (along with many English versions, e.g., NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT) follows the LXX in reading вҖҸЧҗЦұЧңЦ№Ч”Ц·ЧҷвҖҺ (Кҫelohay, вҖңmy GodвҖқ) rather than MTвҖҷs вҖҸЧҗЦұЧңЦ№Ч”ЦөЧҷвҖҺ (Кҫelohe, вҖңthe God ofвҖқ). See Ps 18:2.
n1002210:
tn Heb вҖңthe horn of my salvation,вҖқ or вҖңmy saving horn.вҖқ
sn Though some see вҖңhornвҖқ as referring to a horn-shaped peak of a hill, or to the вҖңhornsвҖқ of an altar where one could find refuge, it is more likely that the horn of an ox underlies the metaphor (see Deut 33:17; 1 Kgs 22:11; Ps 92:10). The horn of the wild ox is frequently a metaphor for military strength; the idiom вҖңexalt the hornвҖқ signifies military victory (see 1 Sam 2:10; Pss 89:17, 24; 92:10; Lam 2:17). In the ancient Near East powerful warrior-kings would sometimes compare themselves to a goring bull that uses its horns to kill its enemies. For examples, see P. Miller, вҖңEl the Warrior,вҖқ HTR 60 (1967): 422-25, and R. B. Chisholm, вҖңAn Exegetical and Theological Study of Psalm 18/2 Samuel 22вҖқ (Th.D. diss., Dallas Theological Seminary, 1983), 135-36. 2 Sam 22:3 uses the metaphor of the horn in a slightly different manner. Here the Lord himself is compared to a horn. He is to the psalmist what the horn is to the ox, a source of defense and victory.
n1002211: tn The parallel version of the song in Ps 18 does not include this last line.
n1002212: tn In this song of thanksgiving, where David recalls how the Lord delivered him, the prefixed verbal form is best understood as a preterite indicating past tense (cf. CEV вҖңI prayedвҖқ), not an imperfect (as in many English versions).
n1002213: tn Heb вҖңworthy of praise, I cried out [to] the Lord.вҖқ Some take вҖҸЧһЦ°Ч”Ц»ЧңЦјЦёЧңвҖҺ (mehullal, вҖңworthy of praiseвҖқ) with what precedes and translate, вҖңthe praiseworthy one,вҖқ or вҖңpraiseworthy.вҖқ However, the various epithets in vv. 1-2 have the first person pronominal suffix, unlike вҖҸЧһЦ°Ч”Ц»ЧңЦјЦёЧңвҖҺ. If one follows the traditional verse division and takes вҖҸЧһЦ°Ч”Ц»ЧңЦјЦёЧңвҖҺ with what follows, it is best understood as substantival and as appositional to вҖҸЧҷЦ°Ч”Ч•ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (yehvah, вҖңYahwehвҖқ), resulting in вҖң[to the] praiseworthy one I cried out, [to the] Lord.вҖқ
n1002214: tn The noun вҖҸЧ Ц·Ч—Ц·ЧңвҖҺ (nakhal) usually refers to a river or stream, but in this context the plural form likely refers to the currents of the sea (see vv. 15-16).
n1002215: tn The noun вҖҸЧ‘Ц°ЧңЦҙЧҷЦјЦ·ЧўЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (beliyyaКҝal) is used here as an epithet for death. Elsewhere it is a common noun meaning вҖңwickedness, uselessnessвҖқ (see HALOT 133-34 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°ЧңЦҙЧҷЦјЦ·ЧўЦ·ЧңвҖҺ). It is often associated with rebellion against authority and other crimes that result in societal disorder and anarchy. The phrase вҖңman/son of wickednessвҖқ refers to one who opposes God and the order he has established. The term becomes an appropriate title for death, which, through human forces, launches an attack against GodвҖҷs chosen servant.
n1002216: tn In this poetic narrative context the prefixed verbal form is best understood as a preterite indicating past tense, not an imperfect. (Note the perfect verbal form in the parallel/preceding line.) The verb вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧўЦ·ЧӘвҖҺ (baКҝat) sometimes by metonymy carries the nuance вҖңfrighten,вҖқ but the parallelism (note вҖңengulfedвҖқ in the preceding line) favors the meaning вҖңoverwhelmвҖқ here.
n1002217: tn вҖңSheol,вҖқ personified here as DavidвҖҷs enemy, is the underworld, place of the dead in primitive Hebrew cosmology.
n1002220: tn In this poetic narrative the two prefixed verbal forms in v. 7a are best understood as preterites indicating past tense, not imperfects. Note the use of the vav consecutive with the prefixed verbal form that follows in v. 7b.
n1002221: tn Heb вҖңfrom his temple.вҖқ Verse 10, which pictures God descending from the sky, indicates that the heavenly, not earthly, temple is in view.
n1002222: tn Heb вҖңand my cry for help [entered] his ears.вҖқ
n1002223: tn The earth heaved and shook. The imagery pictures an earthquake, in which the earthвҖҷs surface rises and falls. The earthquake motif is common in Old Testament theophanies of God as warrior and in ancient Near eastern literary descriptions of warring gods and kings. See R. B. Chisholm, вҖңAn Exegetical and Theological Study of Psalm 18/2 Samuel 22вҖқ (Th.D. diss., Dallas Theological Seminary, 1983), 160-62.
n1002224: tn Psalm 18:7 reads вҖңthe roots of the mountains.вҖқ
n1002225: tn In this poetic narrative context the prefixed verbal form is best understood as a preterite indicating past tense, not an imperfect. Note the three prefixed verbal forms with vav consecutive in the verse.
n1002226: tn Heb вҖңwithinвҖқ or вҖң[from] within.вҖқ For a discussion of the use of the preposition вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ (bet) here, see R. B. Chisholm, вҖңAn Exegetical and Theological Study of Psalm 18/2 Samuel 22вҖқ (Th.D. diss., Dallas Theological Seminary, 1983), 163-64.
n1002227: tn Or вҖңin his anger.вҖқ The noun вҖҸЧҗЦ·ЧЈвҖҺ (Кҫaf) can carry the abstract meaning вҖңanger,вҖқ but the parallelism (note вҖңfrom his mouthвҖқ) suggests the more concrete meaning вҖңnoseвҖқ here (most English versions, вҖңnostrilsвҖқ). See also v. 16, вҖңthe powerful breath of your nose.вҖқ
n1002228:
tn Heb вҖңfire from his mouth devoured.вҖқ In this poetic narrative the prefixed verbal form is best understood as a preterite indicating past tense, not an imperfect. Note the two perfect verbal forms in the verse.
sn For other examples of fire as a weapon in Old Testament theophanies and ancient Near Eastern portrayals of warring gods and kings, see R. B. Chisholm, вҖңAn Exegetical and Theological Study of Psalm 18/2 Samuel 22вҖқ (Th.D. diss., Dallas Theological Seminary, 1983), 165-67.
n1002229: tn Heb вҖңcoals burned from him.вҖқ Perhaps the psalmist pictures GodвҖҷs fiery breath igniting coals (see Job 41:21), which he then hurls as weapons (see Ps 120:4).
n1002230: tn The verb вҖҸЧ ЦёЧҳЦёЧ”вҖҺ (natah) can carry the sense вҖң[to cause to] bend; [to cause to] bow downвҖқ (see HALOT 693 s.v. вҖҸЧ ЦёЧҳЦёЧ”вҖҺ). For example, Gen 49:15 pictures Issachar as a donkey that вҖңbendsвҖқ its shoulder or back under a burden (cf. KJV, NASB, NRSV вҖңHe bowed the heavensвҖқ; NAB вҖңHe inclined the heavensвҖқ). Here the Lord causes the sky, pictured as a dome or vault, to bend or sink down as he descends in the storm.
n1002232:
tn Heb вҖңa cherubвҖқ (so KJV, NAB, NRSV); NIV вҖңthe cherubimвҖқ (plural); TEV вҖңhis winged creatureвҖқ; CEV вҖңflying creatures.вҖқ
sn A winged angel. Cherubim, as depicted in the Old Testament, possess both human and animal (lion, ox, and eagle) characteristics (see Ezek 1:10; 10:14, 21; 41:18). They are pictured as winged creatures (Exod 25:20; 37:9; 1 Kgs 6:24-27; Ezek 10:8, 19) and serve as the very throne of God when the ark of the covenant is in view (Pss 80:1; 99:1; see Num 7:89; 1 Sam 4:4; 2 Sam 6:2; 2 Kgs 19:15). The picture of the Lord seated on the cherubim suggests they might be used by him as a vehicle, a function they carry out in Ezek 1:22-28 (the вҖңliving creaturesвҖқ mentioned here are identified as cherubim in Ezek 10:20). In Ps 18:10 the image of a cherub serves to personify the wind (see the next line).
n1002233: tc The translation follows very many medieval Hebrew mss in reading вҖҸЧ•Ц·ЧҷЦјЦөЧ“Ц¶ЧҗвҖҺ (vayyedeКҫ, вҖңand he glidedвҖқ; cf. NIV вҖңsoaredвҖқ; NCV вҖңracedвҖқ) rather than MT вҖҸЧ•Ц·ЧҷЦјЦөЧЁЦёЧҗвҖҺ (vayyeraКҫ, вҖңand he appeared,вҖқ so NASB, CEV). See as well the Syriac Peshitta, Targum, Vulgate, and the parallel version in Ps 18:10, which preserves the original reading (see the note there).
n1002234: sn The wings of the wind. Verse 10 may depict the Lord mounting a cherub, which is in turn propelled by the wind current. Another option is that two different vehicles (a cherub and the wind) are envisioned. A third option is that the wind is personified as a cherub. For a discussion of ancient Near Eastern parallels to the imagery in v. 10, see M. Weinfeld, вҖңвҖҳRider of the CloudsвҖҷ and вҖҳGatherer of the CloudsвҖҷ,вҖқ JANESCU 5 (1973): 422-24.
n1002235: tc Heb вҖңhe made darkness around him coverings.вҖқ The parallel text in Ps 18:11 reads вҖңhe made darkness his hiding place around him, his covering.вҖқ 2 Sam 22:12 omits вҖңhis hiding placeвҖқ and pluralizes вҖңcovering.вҖқ Psalm 18:11 may include a conflation of synonyms (вҖңhis hiding placeвҖқ and вҖңhis coveringвҖқ) or 2 Sam 22:12 may be the result of haplography/homoioarcton. Note that three successive words in Ps 18:11 begin with the letter вҖҸЧЎвҖҺ (samek): вҖҸ ЧЎЦҙЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ ЧЎЦ°Ч‘ЦҙЧҷЧ‘Ч•Ц№ЧӘЦёЧҷЧ• ЧЎЦ»ЧӣЦјЦёЧӘЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (sitro sevivotayv sukkato).
n1002236: tc Heb вҖңa sieve of water, clouds of clouds.вҖқ The form вҖҸЧ—Ц·Ч©ЧҒЦ°ЧЁЦ·ЧӘвҖҺ (khashrat) is a construct of вҖҸЧ—Ц·Ч©ЧҒЦ°ЧЁЦёЧ”вҖҺ (khashrah, вҖңsieveвҖқ), which occurs only here in the OT. A cognate Ugaritic noun means вҖңsieve,вҖқ and a related verb вҖҸЧ—Ч©ЧҒЧЁвҖҺ (вҖңto siftвҖқ) is attested in post-biblical Hebrew and Aramaic (see HALOT 363 s.v. *вҖҸЧ—Ч©ЧҒЧЁвҖҺ). The phrase вҖҸЧ—Ц·Ч©ЧҒЦ°ЧЁЦ·ЧӘЦҫЧһЦ·ЧҷЦҙЧқвҖҺ (khashrat mayim) means literally вҖңa sieve of water.вҖқ It pictures the rain clouds as a sieve through which the rain falls to the ground. (See F. M. Cross and D. N. Freedman, Studies in Ancient Yahwistic Poetry, 146, note 33.)
n1002237: tc The parallel text in Ps 18:12 reads вҖңfrom the brightness in front of him his clouds came, hail and coals of fire.вҖқ The Lucianic family of texts within the Greek tradition of 2 Sam 22:13 seems to assume the underlying Hebrew text: вҖҸЧһЦҙЧ ЦјЦ№Ч’Ц·Ч”Цј Ч Ц¶Ч’Ц°Ч“ЦјЧ•Ц№ ЧўЦёЧ‘Ц°ЧЁЧ•Цј Ч‘ЦјЦёЧЁЦёЧ“ Ч•Ц°Ч’Ц·Ч—ЦІЧңЦөЧҷ ЧҗЦөЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ (minnogah negdo Кҝaveru barad vegakhale Кҫesh, вҖңfrom the brightness in front of him came hail and coals of fireвҖқ) which is the basis for the present translation. The textual situation is perplexing and the identity of the original text uncertain. The verbs вҖҸЧўЦёЧ‘Ц°ЧЁЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (Кҝaveru; Ps 18:12) and вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧўЦІЧЁЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (baКҝaru, 2 Sam 22:13) appear to be variants involving a transposition of the first two letters. The noun вҖҸЧўЦёЧ‘ЦёЧҷЧ•вҖҺ (Кҝavayv, вҖңhis cloudsвҖқ; Ps 18:12) may be virtually dittographic (note the following вҖҸЧўЦёЧ‘Ц°ЧЁЧ•ЦјвҖҺ), or it could have accidentally dropped from the text of 2 Sam 22:13 by virtual haplography (note the preceding вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧўЦІЧЁЧ•ЦјвҖҺ [baКҝaru], which might have originally read вҖҸЧўЦёЧ‘Ц°ЧЁЧ•ЦјвҖҺ). The term вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧЁЦёЧ“вҖҺ (barad, вҖңhailвҖқ; Ps 18:12) may be virtually dittographic (note the preceding вҖҸЧўЦёЧ‘Ц°ЧЁЧ•ЦјвҖҺ), or it could have dropped from 2 Sam 22:13 by virtual haplography (note the preceding вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧўЦІЧЁЧ•ЦјвҖҺ, which might have originally read вҖҸЧўЦёЧ‘Ц°ЧЁЧ•ЦјвҖҺ). For a fuller discussion of the text, see R. B. Chisholm, вҖңAn Exegetical and Theological Study of Psalm 18/2 Samuel 22вҖқ (Th.D. diss., Dallas Theological Seminary, 1983), 74-76.
n1002238: tn The shortened theme vowel indicates that the prefixed verbal form is a preterite.
n1002239: sn This divine title (вҖҸЧўЦ¶ЧңЦ°ЧҷЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ, Кҝelyon) pictures God as the exalted ruler of the universe who vindicates the innocent and judges the wicked. See especially Ps 47:2.
n1002240:
tn Heb вҖңoffered his voice.вҖқ In this poetic narrative context the prefixed verbal form is best understood as a preterite indicating past tense, not an imperfect. Note the preterite form in the preceding line. The text of Ps 18:13 adds at this point, вҖңhail and coals of fire.вҖқ These words are probably accidentally added from v. 12b; they do not appear in 2 Sam 22:14.
sn Thunder is a common motif in Old Testament theophanies and in ancient Near Eastern portrayals of the storm god and warring kings. See R. B. Chisholm, вҖңAn Exegetical and Theological Study of Psalm 18/2 Samuel 22вҖқ (Th.D. diss., Dallas Theological Seminary, 1983), 179-83.
n1002242: tn The pronominal suffixes on the verbs вҖңscatteredвҖқ and вҖңroutedвҖқ (see the next line) refer to DavidвҖҷs enemies. Some argue that the suffixes refer to the arrows, in which case one might translate вҖңshot them far and wideвҖқ and вҖңmade them move noisily,вҖқ respectively. They argue that the enemies have not been mentioned since v. 4 and are not again mentioned until v. 17. However, usage of the verbs вҖҸЧӨЧ•ЦјЧҘвҖҺ (puts, вҖңscatterвҖқ) and вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧһЦ·ЧқвҖҺ (hamam, вҖңroutвҖқ) elsewhere in Holy War accounts suggests the suffixes refer to enemies. Enemies are frequently pictured in such texts as scattered and/or routed (see Exod 14:24; 23:27; Num 10:35; Josh 10:10; Judg 4:15; 1 Sam 7:10; 11:11; Ps 68:1).
n1002243: sn Lightning is a common motif in OT theophanies and in ancient Near Eastern portrayals of the storm god and warring kings. Arrows and lightning bolts are associated in other texts (see Pss 77:17-18; 144:6; Zech 9:14), as well as in ancient Near Eastern art. See R. B. Chisholm, вҖңAn Exegetical and Theological Study of Psalm 18/2 Samuel 22вҖқ (Th.D. diss., Dallas Theological Seminary, 1983), 187, 190-92.
n1002246: tn The noun is derived from the verb вҖҸЧ’ЦёЧўЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ (gaКҝar) which is often understood to mean вҖңrebuke.вҖқ In some cases it is apparent that scolding or threatening is in view (see Gen 37:10; Ruth 2:16; Zech 3:2). However, in militaristic contexts this translation is inadequate, for the verb refers in this setting to the warriorвҖҷs battle cry, which terrifies and paralyzes the enemy. See A. Caquot, TDOT 3:53, and note the use of the verb in Pss 68:30; 106:9; and Nah 1:4, as well as the related noun in Job 26:11; Pss 9:5; 76:6; 104:7; Isa 50:2; 51:20; 66:15.
n1002247: tn Heb вҖңblast of the breathвҖқ (literally, вҖңbreath of breathвҖқ) employs an appositional genitive. Synonyms are joined in a construct relationship to emphasize the single idea. For a detailed discussion of the grammatical point with numerous examples, see Y. Avishur, вҖңPairs of Synonymous Words in the Construct State (and in Appositional Hendiadys) in Biblical Hebrew,вҖқ Semitics 2 (1971): 17-81.
n1002248: tn Heb вҖңstretched.вҖқ Perhaps вҖңhis handвҖқ should be supplied by ellipsis (see Ps 144:7). In this poetic narrative context the three prefixed verbal forms in this verse are best understood as preterites indicating past tense, not imperfects.
n1002249: tn Heb вҖңmighty waters.вҖқ The waters of the sea symbolize the psalmistвҖҷs powerful enemies, as well as the realm of death they represent (see v. 5 and Ps 144:7).
n1002250: tn The singular refers either to personified death or collectively to the psalmistвҖҷs enemies. The following line, which refers to вҖңthose [plural] who hate me,вҖқ favors the latter.
n1002251: tn The same verb is translated вҖңtrappedвҖқ in v. 6. In this poetic narrative context the prefixed verbal form is best understood as a preterite indicating past tense, not imperfect. Cf. NAB, NCV, TEV, NLT вҖңattacked.вҖқ
n1002253: tn Or вҖңdelighted in meвҖқ (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n1002254: tn In this poetic narrative context the prefixed verbal form is best understood as a preterite indicating past tense, not imperfect.
n1002255: tn Heb вҖңaccording to my righteousness.вҖқ As vv. 22-25 make clear, David refers here to his unwavering obedience to GodвҖҷs commands. He explains that the Lord was pleased with him and willing to deliver him because he had been loyal to God and obedient to his commandments. Ancient Near Eastern literature contains numerous parallels. A superior (a god or king) would typically reward a subject (a king or the servant of a king, respectively) for loyalty and obedience. See R. B. Chisholm, вҖңAn Exegetical and Theological Study of Psalm 18/2 Samuel 22вҖқ (Th.D. diss., Dallas Theological Seminary, 1983), 211-13.
n1002256: tn The unreduced Hiphil prefixed verbal form appears to be an imperfect, in which case the psalmist would be generalizing. However, both the preceding and following contexts (see especially v. 25) suggest he is narrating his experience. Despite its unreduced form, the verb is better taken as a preterite. For other examples of unreduced Hiphil preterites, see Pss 55:14a; 68:9a, 10b; 80:8a; 89:43a; 107:38b; 116:6b.
n1002257: tn Heb вҖңaccording to the purity of my hands he repaid to me.вҖқ Hands suggest activity and behavior.
n1002258: tn Heb вҖңfor I have kept the ways of the Lord.вҖқ The phrase вҖңways of the LordвҖқ refers here to the вҖңconduct requiredвҖқ by the Lord (see HALOT 232 s.v. вҖҸЧ“Ц¶ЧЁЦ¶ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ). In Ps 25 the LordвҖҷs вҖңwaysвҖқ are associated with his covenantal demands (see vv. 4, 9-10). See also Ps 119:3 (cf. vv. 1, 4), as well as Deut 8:6; 10:12; 11:22; 19:9; 26:17; 28:9; 30:16.
n1002259: tn Heb вҖңI have not acted wickedly from my God.вҖқ The statement is elliptical, the idea being, вҖңI have not acted wickedly and, in so doing, departed from my God.вҖқ
n1002260: tn Heb вҖңfor all his regulations are before me.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧһЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦ°ЧӨЦјЦёЧҳЦёЧ•вҖҺ (mishpatav, вҖңhis regulationsвҖқ) refers to GodвҖҷs covenantal requirements, especially those which the king is responsible to follow (cf Deut 17:18-20). See also Pss 19:9 (cf vv. 7-8); 89:30; 147:20 (cf v. 19), as well as the numerous uses of the term in Ps 119.
n1002261: tn Heb вҖңand his rules, I do not turn aside from it.вҖқ Ps 18:22 reads, вҖңand his rules I do not turn aside from me.вҖқ The prefixed verbal form is probably an imperfect; David here generalizes about his loyalty to GodвҖҷs commands. The LordвҖҷs вҖңrulesвҖқ are the stipulations of the covenant which the king was responsible to obey (see Ps 89:31; cf v. 30 and Deut 17:18-20).
n1002262: tn Heb вҖңfrom my sin,вҖқ that is, from making it my own in any way. Leading a вҖңblamelessвҖқ life meant that the king would be loyal to GodвҖҷs covenant, purge the government and society of evil and unjust officials, and reward loyalty to the Lord (see Ps 101).
n1002263: tn Heb вҖңaccording to my righteousness.вҖқ See v. 21.
n1002264: tn Heb вҖңaccording to my purity before his eyes.вҖқ
n1002265: tn The imperfect verbal forms in vv. 26-30 draw attention to GodвҖҷs characteristic actions. Based on his experience, the psalmist generalizes about GodвҖҷs just dealings with people (vv. 26-28) and about the way in which God typically empowers him on the battlefield (vv. 29-30). The Hitpael stem is used in vv. 26-27 in a reflexive resultative (or causative) sense. God makes himself loyal, etc. in the sense that he conducts or reveals himself as such. On this use of the Hitpael stem, see GKC 149-50 В§54.e.
n1002266: tn Or вҖңto a faithful follower.вҖқ A вҖҸЧ—ЦёЧЎЦҙЧҷЧ“вҖҺ (khasid, вҖңfaithful followerвҖқ) is one who does what is right in GodвҖҷs eyes and remains faithful to God (see Pss 4:3; 12:1; 16:10; 31:23; 37:28; 86:2; 97:10).
n1002268: tc Heb вҖңa warrior of innocence.вҖқ The parallel text in Ps 18:25 reads, probably correctly, вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦ¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧЁвҖҺ (gever, вҖңmanвҖқ) instead of вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦҙЧ‘ЦјЧ•Ц№ЧЁвҖҺ (gibbor, вҖңwarriorвҖқ).
n1002270: tc The translation follows two medieval Hebrew mss in reading вҖҸЧӘЦјЦҙЧӘЦ°ЧӨЦјЦ·ЧӘЦјЦёЧңвҖҺ (titpattal) from the root вҖҸЧӨЦјЦёЧӘЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (patal, вҖңto twistвҖқ), rather than the MT вҖҸЧӘЦјЦҙЧӘЦјЦ·ЧӨЦјЦёЧңвҖҺ (tittappal, from the root вҖҸЧӘЦјЦёЧӨЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (tafal, вҖңto be tasteless,вҖқ вҖңbehave sillyвҖқ; cf. KJV вҖңunsavouryвҖқ). See as well the parallel passage in Ps 18:26. The verb вҖҸЧӨЦјЦёЧӘЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (patal) is used in only three other texts. In Gen 30:8 it means literally вҖңto wrestle,вҖқ or вҖңto twist.вҖқ In Job 5:13 it refers to devious individuals, and in Prov 8:8 to deceptive words. Cf. NAB, NASB вҖңastuteвҖқ; NIV вҖңshrewdвҖқ; NRSV вҖңperverseвҖқ; TEV, NLT вҖңhostile.вҖқ
n1002271: tn The adjective вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ§ЦјЦөЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ (Кҝiqqesh) has the basic nuance вҖңtwisted; crooked,вҖқ and by extension refers to someone or something that is morally perverse. It appears frequently in Proverbs, where it is used of evil people (22:5), speech (8:8; 19:1), thoughts (11:20; 17:20) and life styles (2:15; 28:6). A righteous king opposes such people (Ps 101:4). Verses 26-27 affirm GodвҖҷs justice. He responds to people in accordance with their moral character. His response mirrors their actions. The faithful and blameless find God to be loyal and reliable in his dealings with them. But deceivers discover he is able and willing to use deceit to destroy them. For a more extensive discussion of the theme of divine deception in the OT, see R. B. Chisholm, вҖңDoes God Deceive?вҖқ BSac 155 (1998): 11-28.
n1002272: tn Or perhaps вҖңhumbleвҖқ (so NIV, NRSV, NLT; note the contrast with those who are proud).
n1002273: tc Heb вҖңbut your eyes are upon the proud, you bring low.вҖқ Psalm 18:27 reads вҖңbut proud eyes you bring low.вҖқ
n1002274: tn Or вҖңfor.вҖқ The translation assumes that вҖҸЧӣЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (ki) is asseverative here.
n1002275: tc Many medieval Hebrew mss, some LXX mss, and the Syriac Peshitta support reading вҖҸЧӘЦјЦёЧҗЦҙЧҷЧЁвҖҺ (taКҫir, вҖңyou cause to shineвҖқ) before the words вҖңmy lamp.вҖқ See Ps 18:28. The metaphor, which likens the Lord to a lamp or light, pictures him as the psalmistвҖҷs source of life. For other examples of вҖңlampвҖқ used in this way, see Job 18:6; 21:17; Prov 13:9; 20:20; 24:20. For other examples of вҖңlightвҖқ as a symbol for life, see Job 3:20; 33:30; Ps 56:13.
n1002276: tc The Lucianic Greek recension and Vulgate understand this verb to be second person rather than third person as in the MT. But this is probably the result of reading the preceding word вҖңLordвҖқ as a vocative under the influence of the vocative in the first part of the verse.
n1002278: tn Or вҖңfor.вҖқ The translation assumes that вҖҸЧӣЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (ki) is asseverative here.
n1002280: tn Heb вҖңI will run.вҖқ The imperfect verbal forms in v. 30 indicate the subjectвҖҷs potential or capacity to perform an action. Though one might expect a preposition to follow the verb here, this need not be the case with the verb вҖҸЧЁЧ•ЦјЧҘвҖҺ (ruts; see 1 Sam 17:22). Some emend the Qal to a Hiphil form of the verb and translate, вҖңI put to flight [literally, вҖңcause to runвҖқ] an army.вҖқ
n1002281: tn More specifically, the noun refers to a raiding party or to a contingent of troops (see HALOT 177 s.v. II вҖҸЧ’Ц°Ч“Ч•ЦјЧ“вҖҺ). The picture of a divinely empowered warrior charging against an army in almost superhuman fashion appears elsewhere in ancient Near Eastern literature. See R. B. Chisholm, вҖңAn Exegetical and Theological Study of Psalm 18/2 Samuel 22вҖқ (Th.D. diss., Dallas Theological Seminary, 1983), 228.
n1002283: tn David uses hyperbole to emphasize his God-given military superiority.
n1002284: tn Heb вҖң[As for] the God, his way is blameless.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧҗЦөЧңвҖҺ (haКҫel, вҖңthe GodвҖқ) stands as a nominative (or genitive) absolute in apposition to the resumptive pronominal suffix on вҖңway.вҖқ The prefixed article emphasizes his distinctiveness as the one true God (see BDB 42 s.v. II вҖҸЧҗЦөЧңвҖҺ 6; Deut 33:26). GodвҖҷs вҖңwayвҖқ in this context refers to his protective and salvific acts in fulfillment of his promise (see also Deut 32:4; Pss 67:2; 77:13 [note vv. 11-12, 14]; 103:7; 138:5; 145:17).
n1002285: tn Heb вҖңthe word of the Lord is purified.вҖқ The LordвҖҷs вҖңwordвҖқ probably refers here to his oracle(s) of victory delivered to the psalmist before the battle(s) described in the following context. See also Pss 12:5-7 and 138:2-3. David frequently received such oracles before going into battle (see 1 Sam 23:2, 4-5, 10-12; 30:8; 2 Sam 5:19). The LordвҖҷs word of promise is absolutely reliable; it is compared to metal that has been refined in fire and cleansed of impurities. See Ps 12:6. In the ancient Near East kings would typically seek and receive oracles from their god(s) prior to battle. For examples, see R. B. Chisholm, вҖңAn Exegetical and Theological Study of Psalm 18/2 Samuel 22вҖқ (Th.D. diss., Dallas Theological Seminary, 1983), 241-42.
n1002286: tn Or вҖңfor.вҖқ The translation assumes that вҖҸЧӣЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (ki) is asseverative here.
n1002287: tn Heb вҖңrocky cliff,вҖқ which is a metaphor of protection.
n1002288: tn The rhetorical questions anticipate the answer, вҖңNo one.вҖқ In this way the psalmist indicates that the Lord is the only true God and reliable source of protection. See also Deut 32:39, where the Lord affirms that he is the only true God. Note as well the emphasis on his role as protector (вҖҸЧҰЧ•ЦјЧЁвҖҺ, tsur, вҖңrocky cliffвҖқ) in Deut 32:4, 15, 17-18, 30.
n1002289: tn Heb вҖңthe God.вҖқ See the note at v. 31.
n1002290: tc 4QSama has вҖҸЧһЦ°ЧҗЦ·Ч–ЦјЦ°ЧЁЦөЧ ЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (meКҫazzereni, вҖңthe one girding me with strengthвҖқ) rather than the MT вҖҸЧһЦёЧўЧ•ЦјЧ–ЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (maКҝuzzi, вҖңmy refugeвҖқ). See as well Ps 18:32.
n1002291: tn The prefixed verbal form with vav consecutive here carries along the generalizing tone of the preceding line.
n1002292: tn Heb вҖңand he sets free (from the verb вҖҸЧ ЦёЧӘЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ, natar) [the] blameless, his [Kethib; вҖңmyвҖқ (Qere)] way.вҖқ The translation follows Ps 18:32 in reading вҖңhe made my path smooth.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧӘЦјЦёЧһЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (tamim, вҖңsmoothвҖқ) usually carries a moral or ethical connotation, вҖңblameless, innocent.вҖқ However, in Ps 18:33 it refers to a pathway free of obstacles. The reality underlying the metaphor is the psalmistвҖҷs ability to charge into battle without tripping (see vv. 33, 36).
n1002293: tc Heb вҖң[the one who] makes his feet like [those of] a deer.вҖқ The translation follows the Qere and many medieval Hebrew mss in reading вҖҸЧЁЦ·Ч’Ц°ЧңЦ·ЧҷвҖҺ (raglay, вҖңmy feetвҖқ) rather than the MT вҖҸЧЁЦ·Ч’Ц°ЧңЦёЧҷЧ•вҖҺ (raglayv, вҖңhis feetвҖқ). See as well Ps 18:33.
n1002294: tn Heb вҖңand on my high places he makes me walk.вҖқ The imperfect verbal form emphasizes GodвҖҷs characteristic provision. The psalmist compares his agility in battle to the ability of a deer to negotiate rugged, high terrain without falling or being injured. Habakkuk uses similar language to describe his faith during difficult times. See Hab 3:19.
n1002296: tn The psalmist attributes his skill with weapons to divine enabling. Egyptian reliefs picture gods teaching the king how to shoot a bow. See O. Keel, Symbolism of the Biblical World, 265.
n1002297: tn Heb вҖңand a bow of bronze is bent by my arms.вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧ ЦёЧ—ЦөЧӘвҖҺ (nakhet) apparently means вҖңto pull back; to bendвҖқ here (see HALOT 692 s.v. вҖҸЧ Ч—ЧӘвҖҺ). The bronze bow referred to here was probably laminated with bronze strips, or a purely ceremonial or decorative bow made entirely from bronze. In the latter case the language is hyperbolic, for such a weapon would not be functional in battle.
n1002298: tn Another option is to translate the prefixed verb with vav consecutive with a past tense, вҖңyou gave me.вҖқ Several prefixed verbal forms with vav consecutive also appear in vv. 38-44. The present translation understands this section as a description of what generally happened when the author charged into battle, but another option is to understand the section as narrative and translate accordingly.
n1002299:
tc Psalm 18:35 contains an additional line following this one, which reads вҖңyour right hand supports me.вҖқ It may be omitted here due to homoioarcton. See the note at Ps 18:35.
tn Heb вҖңand you give me the shield of your deliveranceвҖқ; KJV, ASV вҖңthe shield of thy (your NRSV, NLT) salvationвҖқ; NIV вҖңyour shield of victory.вҖқ Ancient Near Eastern literature often refers to a god giving a king special weapons. See R. B. Chisholm, вҖңAn Exegetical and Theological Study of Psalm 18/2 Samuel 22вҖқ (Th.D. diss., Dallas Theological Seminary, 1983), 260-61.
n10022100: tn Heb вҖңyour answer makes me great.вҖқ David refers to GodвҖҷs willingness to answer his prayer.
n10022101: tn Heb вҖңstep.вҖқ вҖңStepвҖқ probably refers metonymically to the path upon which the psalmist walks. Another option is to translate, вҖңyou widen my stride.вҖқ This would suggest that God gives him the capacity to run quickly.
n10022102: tn Heb вҖңlower legs.вҖқ On the meaning of the Hebrew noun, which occurs only here, see H. R. Cohen, Biblical Hapax Legomena (SBLDS), 112. A cognate Akkadian noun means вҖңlower leg.вҖқ
n10022103: tn Heb вҖңyou clothed me with strength for battle.вҖқ
n10022104: tn Heb вҖңyou make those who rise against me kneel beneath me.вҖқ
n10022105: tn Heb вҖңand [as for] my enemies, you give to me [the] back [or вҖңneckвҖқ ].вҖқ The idiom вҖңgive [the] backвҖқ means вҖңto cause [one] to turn the back and run away.вҖқ See Exod 23:27 and HALOT 888 s.v. II вҖҸЧўЧЁЧЈвҖҺ.
n10022106: tc The translation follows one medieval Hebrew ms and the ancient versions in reading the Piel вҖҸЧҷЦ°Ч©ЧҒЦ·Ч•ЦјЦ°ЧўЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (yeshavveКҝu, вҖңthey cry for helpвҖқ) rather than the Qal of the MT вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦ°ЧўЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (yishКҝu, вҖңthey look about for helpвҖқ). See Ps 18:41 as well.
n10022108: tn The words вҖңthey cry outвҖқ are not in the Hebrew text. This reference to the psalmistsвҖҷ enemies crying out for help to the Lord suggests that the psalmist refers here to enemies within the covenant community, rather than foreigners. However, the militaristic context suggests foreign enemies are in view. Ancient Near Eastern literature indicates that defeated enemies would sometimes cry out for mercy to the god(s) of their conqueror. See R. B. Chisholm, вҖңAn Exegetical and Theological Study of Psalm 18/2 Samuel 22вҖқ (Th.D. diss., Dallas Theological Seminary, 1983), 271.
n10022109: tn Or вҖңmudвҖқ (so NAB, NIV, CEV). See HALOT 374 s.v. вҖҸЧҳЦҙЧҷЧҳвҖҺ.
n10022110: tn Heb вҖңfrom the strivings of my people.вҖқ In this context вҖҸЧЁЦҙЧҷЧ‘вҖҺ (riv, вҖңstrivingвҖқ) probably has a militaristic sense (as in Judg 12:2; Isa 41:11), and вҖҸЧўЦ·ЧқвҖҺ (Кҝam, вҖңpeopleвҖқ) probably refers more specifically to an army (for other examples, see the verses listed in BDB 766 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦ·ЧқвҖҺ 2.d). The suffix вҖңmyвҖқ suggests David is referring to attacks by his own countrymen, the вҖңpeopleвҖқ being Israel. However, the parallel text in Ps 18:43 omits the suffix.
n10022111: tn Heb вҖңa people whom I did not know serve me.вҖқ In this context the verb вҖңknowвҖқ (вҖҸЧҷЦёЧ“Ц·ЧўвҖҺ, yadaКҝ) probably refers to formal recognition by treaty. People who were once not under the psalmistвҖҷs authority now willingly submit to his rulership to avoid being conquered militarily (see vv. 45-46). The language may recall the events recorded in 2 Sam 8:9-10 and 10:19.
n10022112: tn For the meaning вҖңto be weak; to be powerlessвҖқ for the verb вҖҸЧӣЦјЦёЧ—Ц·Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ (kakhash), see Ps 109:24. Verse 46, which also mentions foreigners, favors this interpretation. Another option is to translate вҖңcower in fearвҖқ (see Deut 33:29; Pss 66:3; 81:15).
n10022113: tn Heb вҖңat a report of an ear they submit to me.вҖқ The report of DavidвҖҷs exploits is so impressive that those who hear it submit to his rulership without putting up a fight.
n10022115: tc The translation assumes a reading вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧҷЦ·Ч—Ц°ЧЁЦ°Ч’Ч•ЦјвҖҺ (veyakhregu, вҖңand they quakedвҖқ) rather than the MT вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧҷЦ·Ч—Ц°Ч’ЦјЦ°ЧЁЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (veyakhgeru, вҖңand they girded themselvesвҖқ). See the note at Ps 18:45.
n10022117: tn Heb вҖңprisons.вҖқ Their besieged cities are compared to prisons.
n10022118: tn Elsewhere the construction вҖҸЧ—Ц·ЧҷЦҫЧҷЦ°Ч”Ч•ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (khay yehvah) is used exclusively as an oath formula, but this is not the case here, for no oath follows. Here the statement is an affirmation of the LordвҖҷs active presence and intervention. In contrast to pagan deities, he demonstrates that he is the living God by rescuing and empowering the psalmist.
n10022119: tn Heb вҖңmy rocky cliff,вҖқ which is a metaphor for protection.
n10022121: tn Heb вҖңthe God of the rock of my deliverance.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧҰЧ•ЦјЧЁвҖҺ (tsur, вҖңrockвҖқ) is probably accidentally repeated from the previous line. The parallel version in Ps 18:46 has simply вҖңthe God of my deliverance.вҖқ
n10022122: tn The words вҖңas kingвҖқ are supplied in the translation for clarification. In the Psalms the verb вҖҸЧЁЧ•ЦјЧқвҖҺ (rum, вҖңbe exaltedвҖқ) when used of God, refers to his exalted position as king (Pss 99:2; 113:4; 138:6) and/or his self-revelation as king through his mighty deeds of deliverance (Pss 21:13; 46:10; 57:5, 11).
n10022123: tn Heb вҖңThe God is the one who grants vengeance to me.вҖқ The plural form of the noun вҖңvengeanceвҖқ indicates degree here, suggesting complete vengeance or vindication. In the ancient Near East military victory was sometimes viewed as a sign that oneвҖҷs God had judged in favor of the victor, avenging and/or vindicating him. See, for example, Judg 11:27, 32-33, 36.
n10022124: tn Heb вҖңand [is the one who] brings down nations beneath me.вҖқ
n10022125: tn Heb вҖңand [the one who] brings me out from my enemies.вҖқ
n10022126: tn Heb вҖңyou lift me up.вҖқ In light of the preceding and following references to deliverance, the verb вҖҸЧЁЧ•ЦјЧқвҖҺ (rum) probably here refers to being rescued from danger (see Ps 9:13). However, it could mean вҖңexalt; elevateвҖқ here, indicating that the Lord has given him victory over his enemies and forced them to acknowledge the psalmistвҖҷs superiority.
n10022127: tn Heb вҖңfrom those who rise against me.вҖқ
n10022128: sn This probably alludes to the fact that David will praise the Lord in the presence of the defeated nations when they, as his subjects, bring their tribute payments. Ideally GodвҖҷs chosen king was to testify to the nations of GodвҖҷs greatness. See J. Eaton, Kingship and the Psalms (SBT), 182-85.
n10022129: tn Heb вҖңto your name.вҖқ GodвҖҷs вҖңnameвҖқ refers metonymically to his divine characteristics as suggested by his name, in this case вҖңLord,вҖқ the primary name of IsraelвҖҷs covenant God which suggests his active presence with his people (see Exod 3:12-15).
n10022130: tc The translation follows the Kethib and the ancient versions in reading вҖҸЧһЦ·Ч’Ц°Ч“ЦјЦҙЧҷЧңвҖҺ (magdil, вҖңhe magnifiesвҖқ) rather than the Qere and many medieval Hebrew mss of the MT which read вҖҸЧһЦҙЧ’Ц°Ч“ЦјЧ•Ц№ЧңвҖҺ (migdol, вҖңtowerвҖқ). See Ps 18:50.
n10022131: tn Heb вҖң[the one who] does loyalty to his anointed one.вҖқ
n100231: tn Heb вҖңthe anointed one of the God of Jacob.вҖқ
n100233: tn Heb вҖңrock,вҖқ used as a metaphor of divine protection.
n100234: tn Heb вҖңFor not thus [is] my house with God?вҖқ
n100235: tn Heb вҖңfor all my deliverance and every desire, surely does he not make [it] grow?вҖқ
n100236: tn Heb вҖңand with fire they are completely burned up in [the place where they] remain.вҖқ The infinitive absolute is used before the finite verb to emphasize that they are completely consumed by the fire.
n100237: tn The Hebrew word is sometimes rendered as вҖңthe three,вҖқ but BDB is probably correct in taking it to refer to military officers (BDB 1026 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ°ЧңЦҙЧҷЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧҷвҖҺ). In that case the etymological connection of this word to the Hebrew numerical adjective for вҖңthreeвҖқ can be explained as originating with a designation for the third warrior in a chariot.
n100238: tc The translation follows some LXX mss (see 1 Chr 11:11 as well) in reading вҖҸЧ”Ч•ЦјЧҗ ЧўЧ•Ц№ЧЁЦөЧЁ ЧҗЦ¶ЧӘЦҫЧ—ЦІЧ ЦҙЧҷЧӘЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (huКҫ Кҝorer Кҫet khanito, вҖңhe raised up his spearвҖқ) rather than the MTвҖҷs вҖҸЧ”Ч•ЦјЧҗ ЧўЦІЧ“ЦҙЧҷЧ Ч•Ц№ Ч”ЦёЧўЦ¶ЧҰЦ°Ч ЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (huКҫ Кҝadino haКҝetsni [Kethib = вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧўЦ¶ЧҰЦ°Ч Ч•Ц№вҖҺ, haКҝetsno]; вҖңAdino the EzeniteвҖқ). The emended text reads literally вҖңhe was wielding his spear against eight hundred, [who were] slain at one time.вҖқ
n1002310: tc This follows the Qere and many medieval Hebrew mss in reading вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦ№Ч“Ч•Ц№вҖҺ (dodo) rather than the Kethib of the MT вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦ№Ч“Ц·ЧҷвҖҺ (doday; cf. ASV, NIV, NLT). But see 1 Chr 27:4.
n1002315: tn The Hebrew text is difficult here. The MT reads вҖҸЧңЦ·Ч—Ц·ЧҷЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ (lachayyah), which implies a rare use of the word вҖҸЧ—Ц·ЧҷЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ (chayyah). The word normally refers to an animal, but if the MT is accepted it would here have the sense of a troop or community of people. BDB 312 s.v. II. вҖҸЧ—Ц·ЧҷЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ, for example, understands the similar reference in v. 13 to be to вҖңa group of allied families, making a raid together.вҖқ But this works better in v. 13 than it does in v. 11, where the context seems to suggest a particular staging location for a military operation. (See 1 Chr 11:15.) It therefore seems best to understand the word in v. 11 as a place name with вҖҸЧ”вҖҺ (he) directive. In that case the Masoretes mistook the word for the common term for an animal and then tried to make sense of it in this context.
n1002317: tn The meaning of Hebrew вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧңЦҫЧ§ЦёЧҰЦҙЧҷЧЁвҖҺ (Кҫel qatsir) seems here to be вҖңat the time of harvest,вҖқ although this is an unusual use of the phrase. As S. R. Driver points out, this preposition does not normally have the temporal sense of вҖңinвҖқ or вҖңduringвҖқ (S. R. Driver, Notes on the Hebrew Text and the Topography of the Books of Samuel, 366).
n1002318: tc The translation follows the Qere and many medieval Hebrew mss in reading вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ°ЧңЦ№Ч©ЧҒЦёЧ”вҖҺ (sheloshah, вҖңthreeвҖқ) rather than the Kethib of the MT вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ°ЧңЦ№Ч©ЧҒЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (sheloshim, вҖңthirtyвҖқ). вҖңThirtyвҖқ is due to dittography of the following word and makes no sense in the context.
n1002321: tn Heb вҖңFar be it to me, O Lord, from doing this.вҖқ
n1002322: tn Heb вҖң[Is it not] the blood of the men who were going with their lives?вҖқ
n1002323: tn Heb вҖңThese things the three warriors did.вҖқ
n1002324: tc The translation follows the Qere, many medieval Hebrew mss, the LXX, and Vulgate in reading вҖҸЧ”Ц·Ч©ЧҒЦјЦ°ЧңЦ№Ч©ЧҒЦёЧ”вҖҺ (hashelosha, вҖңthe threeвҖқ) rather than the Kethib of the MT вҖҸЧ”Ц·Ч©ЧҒЦјЦёЧңЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (hashalishi, вҖңthe third,вҖқ or вҖңadjutantвҖқ). Two medieval Hebrew mss and the Syriac Peshitta have вҖңthirty.вҖқ
n1002325: tn Heb вҖңand he was wielding his spear against three hundred, [who were] slain, and to him there was a name among the three.вҖқ
n1002327: tc The translation follows the Qere and many medieval Hebrew mss in reading вҖҸЧ—Ц·ЧҷЦҙЧңвҖҺ (khayil, вҖңvalorвҖқ) rather than the Kethib of the MT, вҖҸЧ—Ц·ЧҷвҖҺ (khay, вҖңlifeвҖқ).
n1002328: tc Heb вҖңthe two of Ariel, Moab.вҖқ The precise meaning of вҖҸЧҗЧЁЧҷЧҗЧңвҖҺ is uncertain; some read вҖңwarrior.вҖқ The present translation assumes that the word is a proper name and that вҖҸЧ‘Ч ЧҷвҖҺ, вҖңsons of,вҖқ has accidentally dropped from the text by homoioarcton (note the preceding вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ ЧҷвҖҺ), so originally: вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ°Ч ЦөЧҷ Ч‘Ц°Ч ЦөЧҷ ЧҗЦІЧЁЦҙЧҷЧҗЦөЧңвҖҺ (shene vene КҫariКҫel).
n1002329: tc The translation follows the Qere and many medieval Hebrew mss in reading вҖҸЧҗЦҙЧҷЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ (Кҫish, вҖңmanвҖқ) rather than the Kethib of the MT, вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ (Кҫasher, вҖңwhoвҖқ).
n1002333: tc The translation follows many medieval Hebrew mss in reading вҖҸЧ—ЦөЧңЦ¶Ч“вҖҺ (kheled; cf. NAB, NIV, NLT) rather than the MT вҖҸЧ—ЦөЧңЦ¶Ч‘вҖҺ (khelev).
n1002334: tn The Hebrew text does not have вҖңthe son of.вҖқ
n1002336: tc The translation follows the Qere and many medieval Hebrew mss in reading вҖҸЧ—Ц¶ЧҰЦ°ЧЁЦ·ЧҷвҖҺ (khetsray; cf. KJV, NAB) rather than the Kethib of the MT, вҖҸЧ—Ц¶ЧҰЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (khetsro).
n1002337: tc The translation follows the Qere and many medieval Hebrew mss in reading the singular rather than the plural of the Kethib of the MT.
n100241: sn The parallel text in 1 Chr 21:1 says, вҖңAn adversary opposed Israel, inciting David to count how many warriors Israel had.вҖқ The Samuel version gives an underlying theological perspective, while the Chronicler simply describes what happened from a human perspective. The adversary in 1 Chr 21:1 is likely a human enemy, probably a nearby nation whose hostility against Israel pressured David into numbering the people so he could assess his military strength. See the note at 1 Chr 21:1.
n100242: tn Heb вҖңand the word of the king was stronger than.вҖқ
n100247: tn Heb вҖңand Joab gave the number of the numbering of the people.вҖқ
n100248: tn Heb вҖңand the heart of David struck him.вҖқ
n100249: tc The LXX has here вҖңthreeвҖқ rather than вҖңseven,вҖқ and is followed by NAB, NIV, NCV, NRSV, TEV, NLT. See 1 Chr 21:12.
n1002411: tn Heb вҖңThere is great distress to me. Let us fall into the hand of the Lord, for great is his mercy, but into the hand of man let me not fall.вҖқ
n1002415: tn Heb вҖңlet your hand be against me and against the house of my father.вҖқ
n1002416: tn Heb вҖңAraunah.вҖқ The name has been replaced in the translation by the pronoun (вҖңheвҖқ) for stylistic reasons.
n1002418: tn Heb вҖңwhat is good in his eyes.вҖқ
n1002419: sn Threshing sledges were heavy boards used in ancient times for loosening grain from husks. On the bottom sides of these boards sharp stones were embedded, and the boards were then dragged across the grain on a threshing floor by an ox or donkey.
n1002421: tc The Hebrew text is difficult here. The translation reads вҖҸЧўЦ¶Ч‘Ц¶Ч“ ЧҗЦІЧ“Ц№Ч ЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (Кҝeved Кҫadoni, вҖңthe servant of my lordвҖқ) rather than the MTвҖҷs вҖҸЧҗЦІЧЁЦ·Ч•Ц°Ч ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (КҫAravnah). In normal court etiquette a subject would not use his own name in this way, but would more likely refer to himself in the third person. The MT probably first sustained loss of вҖҸЧўЦ¶Ч‘Ц¶Ч“вҖҺ (Кҝeved, вҖңservantвҖқ), leading to confusion of the word for вҖңmy lordвҖқ with the name of the Jebusite referred to here.
n1002422: tn Heb вҖңfifty shekels of silver.вҖқ This would have been about 20 ounces (568 grams) of silver by weight.
